Speed
Luiz Bressane, 44 anos
Defensor Público — Câmara Criminal, Fórum de Barra Funda, SP
São Paulo / Rio de Janeiro
B2 — Upper-Intermediate
General + Legal English (70/30)
Online (Zoom) · 60 min/aula
64 aulas em 8 blocos
General + Legal English — B2 Track

Jornada de Transformação

De: Profissional com bom domínio estrutural do inglês, mas com dificuldade em articular argumentos complexos, participar de discussões acadêmicas/jurídicas e se expressar com fluência e precisão em contextos formais internacionais.

Para: Defensor público capaz de apresentar argumentos jurídicos em inglês com clareza e sofisticação, participar ativamente de simpósios internacionais, debater questões de direito comparado e produzir textos acadêmicos — com confiança e registro adequado ao contexto.

Promessa Transformadora

Ao final das 64 aulas, Luiz será capaz de conduzir argumentações jurídicas em inglês com fluência e precisão, interagir em confêrencias internacionais com autonomia, e redigir textos formais que demonstrem domínio do registro acadêmico-jurídico.

Forças

  • Sólida base gramátical e vocabulário intermediário
  • Forte motivação para atuar internacionalmente
  • Conhecimento jurídico profundo que facilita transferência de conteúdo
  • Experiência prévia com conferências internacionais
  • Disciplina e consistência nos estudos

Desafios

  • Dificuldade em articular argumentos longos e complexos em tempo real
  • Registro formal vs informal ainda não consolidado
  • Hesitação em debates e discussões acadêmicas
  • Vocabulário jurídico em inglês ainda limitado para nível avançado
  • Compreensão auditiva de falantes nativos em velocidade natural

Welcome, Luiz!

Welcome to your B2 Upper-Intermediate journey. Before each class, complete these exercises to prepare your vocabulary, grammar, and pronúnciation. If you ever need a phrase during class, use these emergency expressions:

1Could you elaborate on that point?Poderia elaborar esse ponto?
2I see what you mean, but I would argue that...Entendo o que quer dizer, mas eu argumentaria que...
3If I understand correctly, you are saying that...Se entendi corretamente, você está dizendo que...
4That raises an interesting question about...Isso levanta uma questão interessante sobre...
5Let me put it another way.Deixe-me colocar de outra forma.
Aula 01 — Pre-class

The Weight of Words — Diagnostic + Complex Professional Identity

Present Perfect Simple vs Continuous · Legal vocabulary for court proceedings and advocacy
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary
Acquittal·A formal declaration that someone is not guilty of a criminal charge.
"The jury returned a verdict of acquittal after deliberating for three hours."
Burden of proof·The obligation to prove allegations in a legal proceeding.
"The burden of proof lies with the prosecution in criminal cases."
Cross-examination·The questioning of a witness by the opposing party during a trial.
"During cross-examination, the defense attorney challenged the witness."
Due process·The legal requirement that the government must respect all legal rights owed to a person. (Devido processo legal)
"Every defendant has a right to due process under the constitution."
Jurisdiction·The official power to make legal decisions and judgments. (Jurisdição)
"The court ruled that it did not have jurisdiction over the case."
Litigation·The process of taking legal action; a lawsuit or series of lawsuits. (Litígio)
"Complex litigation can take years to resolve in the federal courts."
Overturn·To reverse a previous legal decision or judgment on appeal.
"The appeals court decided to overturn the original ruling."
Plea bargain·An agreement where the defendant pleads guilty to a lesser charge in exchange for a more lenient sentence.
"The defendant accepted a plea bargain to avoid a lengthy trial."
Precedent·A previous case or legal decision that serves as a rule for future similar cases. (Precedente)
"This case will set an important precedent for future rulings."
Ruling·An official decision made by a judge or court.
"The judge issued a ruling that surprised both parties."
Testimony·A formal statement, especially given under oath in a court of law.
"The witness gave compelling testimony during the hearing."
Verdict·The decision made by a jury in a trial regarding whether a defendant is guilty or not guilty. (Veredicto)
"The verdict was announced after five days of deliberation."
Advocate·To publicly support or recommend a cause or policy; also, a person who pleads on behalf of another.
"As a public defender, I advocate for those who cannot afford representation."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each legal term to its correct definition.

Acquittal
Burden of proof
Cross-examination
Overturn
Plea bargain
Precedent
Verdict

Grammar in Context

Grammar

Luiz has been working as a public defender in São Paulo for over twenty years. Throughout his career, he has handled hundreds of criminal cases, including complex homicide trials. Recently, he has been focusing on cases that involve due process violations and precedent-setting rulings. The defense team has been preparing cross-examination questions for a major trial that starts next week. So far, the prosecution has presented its testimony, and now it is the defense's turn. Luiz has already reviewed the evidence, but he has not yet spoken to all the witnesses. He has been advocating for defendants' rights throughout his entire career, and this case could set an important precedent.

1. How long has Luiz been working as a public defender?
A Ten years
B Over twenty years
C Five years
2. What has the defense team been preparing?
A A plea bargain agreement
B The closing argument
C Cross-examination questions
3. Has Luiz spoken to all the witnesses?
A Yes, he has spoken to everyone.
B No, he has not yet spoken to all of them.
C He has never spoken to witnesses.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

Present Perfect Simple vs Present Perfect Continuous

Present Perfect Simple (have/has + past participle) — used for completed actions with a present result, or experiences up to now.

Usado para ações concluídas com resultado no presente, ou experiências até agora.

+I have handled hundreds of cases.
−She has not testified in this type of case before.
?Have you ever overturned a ruling?

Present Perfect Continuous (have/has + been + -ing) — used for ongoing or recently finished actions, emphasizing duration or continuity.

Usado para ações em andamento ou recentemente finalizadas, enfatizando duração ou continuidade.

+I have been working on this case for three months.
−They have not been negotiating a plea bargain.
?Has the judge been considering the jurisdiction issue?

Key difference: Use Simple for results and completed quantity (how many/how much). Use Continuous for process and duration (how long/since when).
Diferença-chave: Use Simple para resultados e quantidade. Use Continuous para processo e duração.

Stage 1.5: Apply the Grammar

Practice

Complete the sentences with the correct form of Present Perfect Simple or Continuous.

"I as a public defender for over twenty years."
"The defense a motion to overturn the ruling."
"We the testimony, but we have not found any inconsistencies."
"The judge the jurisdiction issue for weeks."
"They a plea bargain for the past two weeks."
"The prosecution the burden of proof."

Stage 2: Put It in Order

Order

Order these sentences to form a coherent professional introduction at a legal symposium.

?"Recently, I have been focusing on cases involving due process violations."
?"I have been working in criminal defense for over twenty years."
?"I have been attending international symposiums to exchange ideas with colleagues."
?"Good afternoon. My name is Luiz Bressane, and I am a public defender from São Paulo, Brazil."
?"Throughout my career, I have handled hundreds of cases, including complex homicide trials."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen, then record yourself saying each phrase. Focus on stress and intonation.

I have been working as a public defender for over twenty years.
The prosecution has not yet met the burden of proof.
The appeals court has overturned three verdicts this month.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. A colleague at a symposium asks about your experience. Which is the best response?
A "I am working as a public defender since 2004."
B "I have been working as a public defender since 2004."
C "I worked as a public defender since 2004."
2. You want to describe a completed result. Which sentence is correct?
A "The appeals court has been overturning the ruling yesterday."
B "The appeals court has overturned the ruling."
C "The appeals court has been overturn the ruling."
3. You want to emphasize the ongoing nature of your preparation. Which is correct?
A "We have prepared the cross-examination questions for two weeks."
B "We have been preparing the cross-examination questions for two weeks."
C "We are preparing the cross-examination questions since two weeks."
4. An American attorney asks if you have experience with jury trials. What do you say?
A "Yes, I have handled hundreds of jury trial cases throughout my career."
B "Yes, I have been handling hundreds of jury trial cases throughout my career."
C "Yes, I handled hundreds of cases since I started."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection
Imagine you are at the International Legal Symposium in Washington, D.C. An American attorney introduces herself and asks about your career. Record a 60-second introduction covering: who you are, how long you have been working in law, what types of cases you have handled, and what you have been focusing on recently. Use both Present Perfect Simple and Continuous.

Survival Card — Lesson 1

1I have been practicing law for over twenty years.Tenho práticado direito por mais de vinte anos.
2The prosecution has not met the burden of proof in this case.A acusação não cumpriu o ônus da prova neste caso.
3We have been reviewing the testimony for inconsistencies.Estamos revisando o depoimento em busca de inconsistências.
4The court has set an important precedent with this ruling.O tribunal estabeleceu um precedente importante com esta decisão.
5I would argue that the evidence does not support the verdict.Eu argumentaria que as provas não sustentam o veredicto.
Aula 02 — Pre-class

Building the Case — Describing Professional Experience

Past Simple vs Present Perfect · Legal vocabulary for prosecution, sentencing, and appeals
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary
Prosecute·To conduct legal proceedings against someone in a court of law.
"The district attorney decided to prosecute the suspect for fraud."
Sentence·The punishment assigned by a court after conviction. (Sentença)
"The judge handed down a sentence of fifteen years in prison."
Appeal·A formal request for a higher court to review a decision. (Apelação)
"The defense filed an appeal after the guilty verdict."
Dismiss·To reject or throw out a legal case or claim.
"The judge dismissed the case due to lack of evidence."
Witness·A person who testifies under oath in court. (Testemunha)
"The key witness testified that she saw the defendant at the scene."
Custody·The state of being detained or held by authorities. (Custódia)
"The suspect was taken into custody after the arrest warrant was issued."
Bail·Temporary release from custody in exchange for a financial guarantee. (Fiança)
"The defendant posted bail and was released pending trial."
Statute·A formal written law enacted by a legislature.
"The new statute came into effect last January."
Allegation·An unproven claim that someone has committed a wrongful act. (Alegação)
"The allegations against the company were never proven in court."
Indict·To formally charge someone with a serious crime.
"The grand jury voted to indict the former executive."
Convict·To declare someone guilty of a criminal offense.
"The jury convicted the defendant on all three counts."
Circumstantial·Based on indirect evidence rather than direct proof.
"The prosecution relied heavily on circumstantial evidence."
Mitigate·To make something less severe or serious.
"The defense argued that the circumstances mitigated the severity of the crime."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each legal term to its correct definition.

Prosecute
Sentence
Appeal
Bail
Indict
Circumstantial
Mitigate

Grammar in Context

Grammar

Luiz started his career as a public defender in São Paulo in 2003. In his first year, he handled mostly minor custody disputes and bail hearings. In 2005, he took on a landmark case: a man was indicted for a crime he did not commit. The evidence was entirely circumstantial, and the judge ultimately dismissed the charges. That experience changed his perspective on the justice system. Since then, Luiz has defended hundreds of clients and has worked to reform bail statutes in his state. He has never lost an appeal at the federal level. Last year, a client was convicted based on a single witness testimony. Luiz filed an appeal, and the higher court reduced the sentence significantly. He has always believed that allegations must be proven beyond a reasonable doubt, and he has spent his entire career trying to mitigate injustice in the system.

1. When did Luiz start his career as a public defender?
A In 2005
B In 2003
C In 2002
2. Why was the 2005 indictment dismissed?
A The witness refused to testify.
B The evidence was entirely circumstantial.
C The defendant posted bail and fled.
3. What has Luiz never done at the federal level?
A He has never prosecuted a case.
B He has never filed a motion.
C He has never lost an appeal.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

Past Simple vs Present Perfect

Past Simple (verb + -ed / irregular form) — used for completed actions at a specific time in the past.

Usado para ações concluídas em um momento específico no passado.

+I started my career in 2003.
−The judge did not dismiss the case.
?Did the jury convict the defendant?

Present Perfect (have/has + past participle) — used for experiences, achievements, and actions connected to the present (no specific time).

Usado para experiências, conquistas e ações conectadas ao presente (sem tempo específico).

+I have prosecuted over two hundred cases.
−She has never lost an appeal.
?Have you ever defended a high-profile case?

Key difference: Past Simple = specific time (in 2005, last year, yesterday). Present Perfect = no specific time, connected to now (ever, never, since, already, yet).
Diferença-chave: Past Simple = tempo específico. Present Perfect = sem tempo específico, conectado ao presente.

Stage 1.5: Apply the Grammar

Practice

Complete the sentences with Past Simple or Present Perfect.

"I my career as a legal intern in 2003."
"I over two hundred cases since then."
"Last year, the court three of my cases."
"I on several high-profile indictments throughout my career."
"In 2018, I a landmark bail reform case."
"I an appeal at the federal level."

Stage 2: Put It in Order

Order

Order these sentences to form a coherent career narration, mixing Past Simple and Present Perfect correctly.

?"In 2005, I defended a man who was wrongfully indicted."
?"Since then, I have defended hundreds of clients and have never lost an appeal."
?"I graduated from law school in 2002."
?"The judge dismissed the case because the evidence was circumstantial."
?"I started working as a public defender in 2003."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen, then record yourself saying each phrase. Focus on stress and intonation.

The defendant was convicted, but we appealed the sentence successfully.
I represented a witness who later became a key figure in the investigation.
The prosecution prosecuted the case aggressively, but the jury acquitted the defendant.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. A journalist asks when you started in criminal law. Which is the best answer?
A "I have started my career in 2003."
B "I started my career in 2003."
C "I am starting my career in 2003."
2. You want to describe your overall career achievements without specifying a time. Which is correct?
A "I prosecuted over two hundred cases in my career."
B "I have prosecuted over two hundred cases in my career."
C "I was prosecuting over two hundred cases in my career."
3. You are describing a specific event from last year. Which sentence is correct?
A "Last year, a client was convicted based on circumstantial evidence."
B "Last year, a client has been convicted based on circumstantial evidence."
C "Last year, a client has convicted based on circumstantial evidence."
4. You want to express that something has never happened in your career. Which is correct?
A "I never lost an appeal at the federal level."
B "I have never lost an appeal at the federal level."
C "I did not never lose an appeal at the federal level."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection
Imagine Sarah Chen, a journalist from The Atlantic, is interviewing you about your career. Record a 90-second response covering: when you started in law (Past Simple), what you have accomplished since then (Present Perfect), and a specific case that changed your perspective (Past Simple). Try to use at least 5 of the new vocabulary words.

Survival Card — Lesson 2

1The defendant posted bail yesterday and has already left the country.O réu pagou fiança ontem e já deixou o país.
2The grand jury indicted the suspect, but the case was dismissed last month.O grande júri indiciou o suspeito, mas o caso foi arquivado no mês passado.
3I have defended clients in over fifty custody hearings throughout my career.Eu defendi clientes em mais de cinquenta audiências de custódia ao longo da minha carreira.
4The witness changed her testimony, and the judge reduced the sentence.A testemunha mudou seu depoimento, e o juiz reduziu a sentença.
5Circumstantial evidence alone has never been enough to convict in my experience.Evidência circunstancial sozinha nunca foi suficiente para condenar na minha experiência.
Aula 03 — Pre-class

The System — Comparing Legal Systems

Relative Clauses (Defining vs Non-defining) · Legal vocabulary for comparative law
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary
Plaintiff·A person who brings a case against another in court.
"The plaintiff filed a lawsuit against the manufacturer for damages."
Defendant·A person accused of a crime or sued in court. (Réu)
"The defendant denied all charges and entered a plea of not guilty."
Judiciary·The branch of government responsible for interpreting laws. (Judiciário)
"An independent judiciary is essential for a functioning democracy."
Tort·A wrongful act leading to civil liability.
"A tort is a civil wrong that causes harm to another person."
Liable·Legally responsible for something. (Responsável legalmente)
"The company was found liable for the environmental damage."
Statutory·Relating to or required by a statute or law.
"The statutory age for voting is eighteen in most countries."
Amend·To make changes to a legal document or law. (Emendar)
"Congress voted to amend the constitution to protect civil rights."
Extradite·To hand over a person accused of a crime to another jurisdiction.
"The government agreed to extradite the suspect to face trial abroad."
Arbitration·A method of resolving disputes outside of court. (Arbitração)
"The two companies resolved their dispute through arbitration."
Codify·To arrange laws into a systematic code.
"Napoleon was the first to codify French law into a unified system."
Common law·Law derived from custom and judicial decisions rather than statutes.
"Common law relies on judicial precedent rather than written statutes."
Habeas corpus·A legal order requiring a person to be brought before a judge. (Habeas corpus)
"The lawyer filed a habeas corpus petition to challenge the detention."
Sovereignty·Supreme authority of a state to govern itself. (Soberania)
"National sovereignty means a state has the right to govern itself."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its correct definition.

Plaintiff
Liable
Codify
Tort
Arbitration
Sovereignty
Common law

Grammar in Context

Grammar

At the Washington symposium, Luiz joins a comparative law panel with Professor James Crawford, who teaches constitutional law at Georgetown University. The panel, which focuses on differences between legal traditions, attracts scholars from around the world. Common law, which originated in England, relies on judicial precedent and case decisions. Countries that follow the civil law tradition, such as Brazil and France, prefer to codify their laws into comprehensive codes. The judiciary in civil law systems interprets the written code, while judges who serve in common law courts create binding decisions. Professor Crawford explains how tort law in the United States has evolved through case law, whereas in Brazil, liability is defined by statutory provisions. Arbitration, which is growing as an alternative to litigation, is used in both systems. The panel also discusses habeas corpus, which protects individuals from unlawful detention, and the role of national sovereignty in deciding whether to extradite suspects. Both speakers agree that the ability to amend the constitution is essential for legal evolution. The plaintiff in a civil case and the defendant in a criminal case both rely on a fair and independent judicial system.

1. What type of legal tradition does common law represent?
A A system based on codified statutes
B A system based on judicial precedent and case decisions
C A system based on international arbitration
2. What does "which" add to the sentence "Arbitration, which is growing as an alternative to litigation, is used in both systems"?
A It restricts the meaning to a specific type of arbitration.
B It adds extra, non-essential information about arbitration.
C It defines the word arbitration for the first time.
3. Why is national sovereignty relevant to extradition?
A Because a state has the authority to decide whether to hand over a suspect to another jurisdiction.
B Because sovereignty prevents any form of international cooperation.
C Because extradition is only available in common law systems.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

Relative Clauses — Defining vs Non-defining

Defining Relative Clauses (who/which/that) — restrict meaning; they identify which person or thing we mean. No commas.

Orações restritivas — identificam qual pessoa ou coisa queremos dizer. Sem vírgulas.

PeopleJudges who serve on the Supreme Court interpret the constitution.
ThingsCountries that follow common law rely on judicial precedent.
ThingsThe statute which governs extradition was amended last year.

Non-defining Relative Clauses (who/which) — add extra information that can be removed without changing the meaning. With commas. Cannot use that.

Orações explicativas — adicionam informação extra que pode ser removida. Com vírgulas. Não se usa "that".

PeopleProfessor Crawford, who teaches at Georgetown, is an expert on comparative law.
ThingsArbitration, which is growing in popularity, resolves disputes outside court.
ThingsHabeas corpus, which protects individual freedom, exists in both traditions.

Key difference: Defining = essential (no commas, can use that). Non-defining = extra info (commas, NEVER that).
Diferença-chave: Restritiva = essencial (sem vírgulas, pode usar "that"). Explicativa = informação extra (com vírgulas, NUNCA "that").

Stage 1.5: Apply the Grammar

Practice

Complete the sentences with who, which, or that.

"Judges serve on constitutional courts have the power to review legislation."
"Arbitration, is a popular alternative to litigation, helps resolve disputes efficiently."
"Countries codify their laws into comprehensive codes follow the civil law tradition."
"In common law systems, originated in England, courts rely on judicial precedent."
"A plaintiff suffers harm can file a tort claim against the defendant."
"Habeas corpus, is guaranteed by the constitution, protects individuals from unlawful detention."

Stage 2: Practice — Ordering

Order

Put these steps in the correct order to describe how a comparative law panel works.

? The panelists compare how tort law and liability differ between systems.
? The moderator introduces the panel and its focus on comparative legal systems.
? The panel concludes with a discussion on sovereignty and extradition.
? Each speaker presents how their country's judiciary interprets the law.
? The audience asks questions about arbitration and habeas corpus.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking
In common law systems, which rely on judicial precedent, courts play a central role in shaping the law.
A plaintiff who suffers harm can file a tort claim against the defendant.
Arbitration, which is growing in popularity, offers an alternative to traditional litigation.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. A colleague asks you to explain the difference between common law and civil law. What do you say?
A "Common law uses codified statutes, while civil law relies on precedent."
B "Common law relies on judicial precedent, while civil law is based on codified statutes."
C "Both systems are identical in how they interpret the law."
2. An American attorney says: "The plaintiff, who filed the case last year, is seeking damages." What type of relative clause is used?
A Defining — it restricts which plaintiff we mean.
B Non-defining — it adds extra information (commas present).
C Neither — it is a conditional clause.
3. You need to explain why Brazil cannot simply hand over a suspect to another country. Which concept do you reference?
A Arbitration — resolving disputes outside of court.
B Tort — a wrongful act leading to civil liability.
C Sovereignty — a state's supreme authority to govern itself.
4. A panelist says: "Judges that create binding decisions shape the common law." Is this sentence correct?
A Yes — "that" is acceptable in a defining relative clause.
B No — "that" can never be used for people.
C No — you must always use commas with relative clauses.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection
Imagine you are at the Washington symposium explaining how the Brazilian legal system works to an American colleague. Record a 90-second response covering: the role of the judiciary in Brazil (defining clause with "who"), how arbitration works differently than in the US (non-defining clause with "which"), and why sovereignty matters for extradition cases. Try to use at least 6 of the new vocabulary words.

Survival Card — Lesson 3

1The judiciary, which interprets the law, plays a crucial role in both systems.O judiciário, que interpreta a lei, desempenha um papel crucial em ambos os sistemas.
2A tort is a wrongful act for which the responsible party may be held liable.Um tort é um ato ilícito pelo qual a parte responsável pode ser responsabilizada.
3The government refused to extradite the suspect, citing national sovereignty.O governo recusou-se a extraditar o suspeito, citando soberania nacional.
4Habeas corpus protects individuals who are unlawfully detained by the state.Habeas corpus protege indivíduos que são detidos ilegalmente pelo Estado.
5Both countries have amended their constitutions to include statutory protections for civil rights.Ambos os países emendaram suas constituições para incluir proteções legais para os direitos civis.
Aula 04 — Pre-class

What If — Hypothetical Legal Scenarios

Second Conditional (If + past simple, would + infinitive) · Criminal justice & ethics vocabulary
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary
Parole·Early release from prison under supervision.
"The prisoner was granted parole after serving ten years of his sentence."
Liberdade condicional
Probation·A period of supervised release instead of prison.
"The judge ordered two years of probation instead of prison time."
Provação / Sursis
Felony·A serious crime punishable by more than one year in prison.
"Armed robbery is classified as a felony in most jurisdictions."
Crime grave / Feloônia
Misdemeanor·A minor crime with lighter punishment.
"Shoplifting under fifty dollars is typically charged as a misdemeanor."
Sanction·A penalty for breaking a law or rule.
"The international community imposed sanctions against the regime."
Sanção
Compliance·The act of following rules, laws, or regulations.
"All financial institutions must ensure compliance with anti-money laundering laws."
Conformidade / Compliance
Fraudulent·Involving deception or dishonesty for personal gain.
"The executive was charged with fraudulent accounting practices."
Negligence·Failure to take reasonable care, resulting in harm.
"Medical negligence can result in significant compensation claims."
Negligência
Compensate·To pay someone for loss, injury, or damage.
"The court ordered the company to compensate the victims for their losses."
Deterrent·Something that discourages a particular action.
"The threat of imprisonment serves as a deterrent against violent crime."
Rehabilitation·The process of restoring someone to a normal life after prison.
"The rehabilitation program helped former inmates reintegrate into society."
Reabilitação
Enforcement·The process of ensuring laws are obeyed.
"Strict enforcement of traffic laws has reduced accidents significantly."
Clemency·Mercy or leniency shown to a convicted person.
"The governor granted clemency to the death-row inmate after new evidence emerged."
Clemência

Stage 1.2: Match the Vocabulary

Practice
Parole
Felony
Sanction
Negligence
Compliance
Rehabilitation
Clemency

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar
During the ethics workshop, Dr. Webb presented a series of hypothetical legal dilemmas. She asked: "If a company failed to ensure compliance with financial regulations, regulators would impose heavy sanctions." She then turned to the question of criminal sentencing: "If the defendant were found guilty of a felony, the judge would consider both deterrence and rehabilitation when determining the sentence." Luiz raised an important point about negligence: "If the evidence proved negligence, the court would order the defendant to compensate the victims." The discussion revealed that hypothetical scenarios are essential tools for exploring the boundaries of justice, enforcement, and clemency.
1. In the passage, why does Dr. Webb use "if a company failed" (past simple) instead of "if a company fails" (present simple)?
A Because the company already failed in the past.
B Because the situation is hypothetical and unlikely, not a real present event.
C Because it describes a future prediction.
2. What structure follows the "if" clause in these hypothetical sentences?
A will + infinitive
B would + infinitive
C had + past participle
3. According to the passage, if the evidence proved negligence, what would happen?
A The defendant would receive clemency.
B The case would be dismissed.
C The court would order the defendant to compensate the victims.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar
Second Conditional — Hypothetical / Unreal Present or Future

Use: To talk about imaginary, hypothetical, or unlikely situations in the present or future.
Uso: Para falar sobre situações hipotéticas, imaginárias ou improváveis no presente ou futuro.

FormStructure
AffirmativeIf + subject + past simple, subject + would + infinitive
NegativeIf + subject + did not + base form, subject + would not + infinitive
QuestionWould + subject + infinitive + if + subject + past simple?

Examples / Exemplos:

  • If the judge granted clemency, the inmate would be released. (Se o juiz concedesse clemência, o preso seria solto.)
  • If we invested in rehabilitation, recidivism would decrease. (Se investíssemos em reabilitação, a reincidência diminuiria.)
  • The company would not face sanctions if it ensured compliance. (A empresa não enfrentaria sanções se garantisse conformidade.)
  • Would you recommend probation if the offense were a misdemeanor? (Você recomendaria provação se a infração fosse uma contravenção?)

Common Mistake: Using "would" in the IF clause.
✗ If I would have more evidence, I would win the case.
✓ If I had more evidence, I would win the case.
NUNCA use "would" na cláusula IF do second conditional.

Stage 1.5: Apply the Grammar

Practice
"If the prisoner showed genuine rehabilitation, the parole board early release."
"If we sentences for misdemeanors, we would have more resources for enforcement."
"If the governor clemency, the inmate would be released from death row."
"If stricter sanctions were imposed, companies their compliance programs."
"If fraudulent behavior unpunished, it would undermine public trust in the system."
"If probation programs were better funded, fewer offenders to crime."

Stage 2: Put the Workshop in Order

Order
? Luiz argues that if the evidence proved negligence, the court would order compensation.
? Dr. Webb welcomes lawyers to the ethics workshop and introduces the first hypothetical scenario.
? Luiz concludes that if he could change one thing, he would invest more in rehabilitation.
? Luiz responds that if he were in that situation, he would advise his client without revealing the confession.
? Dr. Webb asks what Luiz would do if he discovered fraudulent testimony.

Stage 3: Pronunciation Practice

Speaking
If a prisoner showed genuine rehabilitation, the parole board would grant early release.
If we reduced sentences for misdemeanors, we would have more resources for enforcement.
If the governor granted clemency, the inmate would be released from death row.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
A colleague asks: "What would happen if we eliminated prison for all misdemeanors?" Choose the best response using the second conditional.
A "If we will eliminate prison, the courts will save money."
B "If we eliminated prison for misdemeanors, probation programs would become essential."
C "If we had eliminated prison, sanctions would have been different."
A panelist asks: "How would you handle a case involving corporate negligence?" What is the correct second conditional answer?
A "If I would handle that case, I will seek maximum compensation."
B "If I had that case, I would had demanded full compliance."
C "If I handled that case, I would demand full compensation for the victims."
Dr. Webb asks: "If a government refused to enforce anti-fraud laws, what would happen?" Choose the grammatically correct response.
A "If a government refused to enforce those laws, fraudulent behavior would increase dramatically."
B "If a government would refuse enforcement, fraud will increase."
C "If a government refuses, fraud would had increased."
An attorney asks about clemency: "Would you support clemency for a convicted felon who showed genuine rehabilitation?" How would Luiz respond?
A "If the felon will show rehabilitation, I would support clemency."
B "If the felon showed genuine rehabilitation, I would absolutely support clemency."
C "If the felon would show rehabilitation, I will support it."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection
Imagine you are at the ethics workshop with Dr. Webb. She asks you: "If you could change one thing about the criminal justice system in your country, what would it be and why?" Use the second conditional to explain your hypothetical reform. Think about: enforcement, rehabilitation, sanctions, compliance, or clemency.

Suggested response:

"If I could change one thing, I would invest more in rehabilitation programs for offenders. If we focused on rehabilitation instead of punishment alone, fewer people would return to crime. I would also improve enforcement of compliance standards for corporations, because if fraudulent behavior went unpunished, public trust would erode."

Survival Card — Lesson 4

1If fraudulent behavior went unpunished, it would undermine public trust in the justice system.Se o comportamento fraudulento ficasse impune, minaria a confiança pública no sistema de justiça.
2If probation programs were better funded, fewer offenders would return to crime.Se os programas de provação fossem mais bem financiados, menos infratores voltariam ao crime.
3If the court found negligence, the defendant would be ordered to compensate the victims.Se o tribunal constatasse negligência, o réu seria condenado a compensar as vítimas.
4If rehabilitation were prioritized over punishment, society would benefit in the long run.Se a reabilitação fosse priorizada em relação à punição, a sociedade se beneficiaria a longo prazo.
5If enforcement agencies had more resources, compliance rates would increase dramatically.Se as agências de fiscalização tivessem mais recursos, as taxas de conformidade aumentariam drasticamente.
Aula 05 — Pre-class

The Turning Point — Career Decisions

Third Conditional (If + past perfect, would have + past participle) · Criminal justice & career reflection vocabulary
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary
Acquit·To formally declare someone not guilty of a criminal charge.
"The jury voted to acquit the defendant after reviewing the new evidence."
Absolver
Exonerate·To clear someone of blame or wrongdoing, often after new evidence.
"DNA evidence helped exonerate the man who had been wrongfully imprisoned for eighteen years."
Exonerar / Inocentar
Wrongful·Unjust or not legally right.
"The wrongful conviction was overturned after new witnesses came forward."
Incarcerate·To imprison or confine someone.
"The state incarcerated thousands of nonviolent offenders during the 1990s."
Encarcerar
Commute (legal)·To reduce a legal sentence to a less severe one.
"The governor decided to commute the prisoner's life sentence to twenty years."
Recidivism·The tendency to reoffend after being released from prison.
"Studies show that recidivism rates drop when inmates receive education in prison."
Reincidência
Restitution·Compensation paid to a victim for loss or injury.
"The court ordered the defendant to pay restitution to the victims' families."
Restituição / Indenização
Subpoena·An official order to appear in court or produce documents.
"The attorney issued a subpoena to compel the witness to testify."
Arraignment·A court hearing where the accused is formally charged and enters a plea.
"At the arraignment, the accused pleaded not guilty to all charges."
Perjury·The crime of lying under oath in court.
"The witness was charged with perjury after lying under oath."
Perjúrio
Contempt·Disrespect for a court, punishable by fine or imprisonment.
"The judge held the attorney in contempt for refusing to comply with the court order."
Adjudicate·To make a formal judgment or decision on a disputed matter.
"A panel of three judges was appointed to adjudicate the dispute."
Adjudicar / Julgar
Expunge·To officially erase or remove a criminal record.
"After completing his sentence, he applied to have his record expunged."

Stage 1.2: Match the Vocabulary

Practice
Acquit
Incarcerate
Recidivism
Restitution
Perjury
Subpoena
Expunge

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar
During the final roundtable, retired Judge Torres shared a story that moved everyone. She described a case from early in her career: "If I had adjudicated that case differently, an innocent man would not have been incarcerated for two decades." Luiz reflected on his own experience: "If the witness had not committed perjury, my client would never have been wrongfully convicted." He added that "if the defense had issued a subpoena for the surveillance footage, the jury would have acquitted the defendant." Judge Torres agreed: "If we had prioritized reducing recidivism through rehabilitation, we would have seen fewer cases of repeat offenses." The discussion made clear that reflecting on past decisions — and imagining alternative outcomes — is essential for the growth of every legal professional.
1. In the passage, why does Judge Torres use "if I had adjudicated" (past perfect) instead of "if I adjudicated" (past simple)?
A Because she is talking about a hypothetical present situation.
B Because she is imagining a different outcome for something that already happened in the past.
C Because she is making a prediction about a future trial.
2. What structure follows the "if + past perfect" clause in these sentences?
A would + infinitive
B would have + past participle
C will + infinitive
3. According to the passage, what would have happened if the defense had issued a subpoena for the surveillance footage?
A The witness would have committed perjury.
B The jury would have acquitted the defendant.
C The judge would have incarcerated the defendant.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar
Third Conditional — Hypothetical Past Situations

Use: To talk about imaginary situations in the past and their hypothetical results. The event did NOT happen — we are imagining a different past.
Uso: Para falar sobre situações imaginárias no passado e seus resultados hipotéticos. O evento NÃO aconteceu — estamos imaginando um passado diferente.

FormStructure
AffirmativeIf + subject + had + past participle, subject + would have + past participle
NegativeIf + subject + had not + past participle, subject + would not have + past participle
QuestionWould + subject + have + past participle + if + subject + had + past participle?

Examples / Exemplos:

  • If the jury had reviewed the evidence, they would have acquitted the defendant. (Se o júri tivesse revisado as evidências, teria absolvido o réu.)
  • If the witness had not committed perjury, the conviction would not have happened. (Se a testemunha não tivesse cometido perjúrio, a condenação não teria acontecido.)
  • The inmate would have been exonerated sooner if the governor had commuted the sentence. (O preso teria sido inocentado antes se o governador tivesse comutado a sentença.)
  • Would the outcome have been different if the attorney had issued a subpoena? (O resultado teria sido diferente se o advogado tivesse emitido uma intimação?)

Time Expressions / Expressões temporais:

  • back then, at that time, in those days, years ago, before the trial, prior to the verdict

Common Mistake: Using "would of" instead of "would have".
✗ If I had known, I would of helped.
✓ If I had known, I would have helped.
"Would of" NÃO existe em inglês. O som é parecido com "would've" (contração de "would have"), mas a forma escrita correta é SEMPRE "would have".

Stage 1.5: Apply the Grammar

Practice
"If the defense had presented stronger evidence, the jury the defendant."
"If the witness had not committed perjury, the wrongful conviction ."
"If the governor the sentence earlier, the inmate would have been exonerated sooner."
"If the court had ordered restitution, the victims compensation years ago."
"If the attorney had not issued the subpoena, the key evidence hidden."
"If they in rehabilitation programs, recidivism rates would have decreased significantly."

Stage 2: Put the Roundtable in Order

Order
? Luiz shares that if the witness had not committed perjury, his client would never have been wrongfully convicted.
? Judge Torres welcomes everyone to the final roundtable and asks about career-defining moments.
? Luiz concludes that if the justice system had prioritized rehabilitation, recidivism would have decreased.
? Luiz reflects that if he had not become a public defender, he would have pursued academia.
? Judge Torres agrees that having records expunged after exoneration is essential for justice.

Stage 3: Pronunciation Practice

Speaking
If the defense had presented stronger evidence, the jury would have acquitted the defendant.
If the witness had not committed perjury, the wrongful conviction would never have happened.
If the governor had commuted the sentence earlier, the inmate would have been exonerated sooner.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
A colleague at the roundtable asks: "What would have happened if the court had ordered restitution immediately?" Choose the best response using the third conditional.
A "If the court ordered restitution, the victims would receive compensation."
B "If the court had ordered restitution immediately, the victims would have received compensation years ago."
C "If the court will order restitution, the victims will have received compensation."
Judge Torres reflects: "Looking back, I wonder about the arraignment." Which response correctly uses the third conditional?
A "If the arraignment was not rushed, the trial would be fair."
B "If the arraignment would not have been rushed, the conviction would be avoided."
C "If the arraignment had not been rushed, the wrongful conviction could have been avoided."
A panelist asks about recidivism. Which answer uses the third conditional correctly?
A "If they had invested more in rehabilitation, recidivism rates would have decreased significantly."
B "If they invested more in rehabilitation, recidivism rates would decrease."
C "If they would have invested in rehabilitation, recidivism rates would of decreased."
Luiz discusses a past case. Which sentence correctly expresses regret about a past event?
A "If the judge held the lawyer in contempt, the trial would proceed more fairly."
B "If the judge had held the lawyer in contempt earlier, the trial would have proceeded more fairly."
C "If the judge would hold the lawyer in contempt, the trial would have proceeded fairly."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection
Imagine you are at the roundtable with Judge Torres. She asks you: "Looking back on your career, if you had made a different decision at a critical moment, how would your life have been different?" Use the third conditional to describe a hypothetical alternative outcome. Think about: wrongful convictions, career choices, key cases, legal reforms.

Suggested response:

"If I had not taken on that wrongful conviction case early in my career, I would never have understood the importance of fighting for justice. If I had chosen corporate law instead, I would have earned more money, but I would not have helped exonerate innocent people. If the system had invested more in reducing recidivism back then, fewer people would have been incarcerated unjustly."

Survival Card — Lesson 5

1If the attorney had not issued the subpoena, the key evidence would have remained hidden.Se o advogado não tivesse emitido a intimação, a evidência-chave teria permanecido oculta.
2If they had invested in rehabilitation, recidivism rates would have decreased significantly.Se tivessem investido em reabilitação, as taxas de reincidência teriam diminuído significativamente.
3If the record had been expunged, the former inmate would have had a fresh start.Se o registro tivesse sido apagado, o ex-detento teria tido um novo começo.
4If the arraignment had not been rushed, the wrongful conviction could have been avoided.Se a audiência de apresentação não tivesse sido apressada, a condenação injusta poderia ter sido evitada.
5If the judge had held the lawyer in contempt earlier, the trial would have proceeded more fairly.Se o juiz tivesse declarado o advogado em desacato antes, o julgamento teria procedido de forma mais justa.
Aula 06 — Pre-class

Defining Justice — Abstract Concepts

Mixed Conditionals (past→present & present→past) · Philosophy of law vocabulary
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary
Equity·Fairness in the treatment of people under the law.
"True equity requires treating people according to their circumstances, not identically."
Equidade
Impartial·Not favoring one side; unbiased.
"A judge must remain impartial throughout the entire trial."
Imparcial
Accountability·The obligation to accept responsibility for one's actions.
"Accountability is essential for maintaining public trust in the legal system."
Responsabilização
Proportional·Corresponding in size or degree to the situation.
"The sentence should be proportional to the severity of the crime."
Proporcional
Retribution·Punishment as payback for wrongdoing.
"Some argue that retribution is a necessary component of justice."
Retribuição
Restorative·Focused on repairing harm rather than punishing.
"Restorative justice focuses on healing rather than punishment."
Restaurativo
Disproportionate·Too large or small in comparison to what is appropriate.
"The punishment was disproportionate to the offense committed."
Desproporcional
Inherent·Existing as a natural or permanent quality.
"Human dignity is an inherent right that cannot be legislated away."
Inerente
Inalienable·Cannot be taken away or denied.
"The constitution protects certain inalienable rights for all citizens."
Inalienável
Abolish·To formally end a system or practice.
"Many countries have voted to abolish the death penalty."
Abolir
Uphold·To maintain or support a law or principle.
"It is the duty of every court to uphold the rule of law."
Manter / Sustentar
Dissent·Strong disagreement with an official decision.
"Justice Oliveira filed a written dissent arguing the majority opinion was unconstitutional."
Dissenso / Discordância
Fundamental·Of central importance; forming a foundation.
"Access to legal representation is a fundamental right in any democracy."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice
Equity
Retribution
Inherent
Abolish
Proportional
Dissent
Inalienable

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Read the passage and notice the mixed conditional structures in bold.

The debate between retributive and restorative justice has shaped legal systems for centuries. Many scholars argue that if the court had abolished purely punitive approaches decades ago, the system would be more equitable today. Others contend that if justice were truly impartial, fewer wrongful convictions would have occurred throughout history.

Professor Amara Osei from the University of Ghana studies how different cultures define justice. She argues that if accountability were built into every institution, public trust would never have eroded so deeply. Her research shows that if the legislature had upheld early reform proposals, citizens would have greater protections now.

In Brazil, Luiz Bressane has witnessed disproportionate sentencing firsthand. He believes that if restorative justice were the norm, many victims would have found closure years ago. The tension between retribution and restoration remains fundamental to how we understand justice itself.

1. According to the text, what would have happened if courts had abolished purely punitive approaches?
A Crime rates would have increased.
B The system would be more equitable today.
C All prisoners would have been released.
2. What type of conditional is "If justice were truly impartial, fewer wrongful convictions would have occurred"?
A Third conditional (past → past)
B Mixed conditional (present → past)
C Second conditional (present → present)
3. What does Professor Osei believe about accountability?
A It is unnecessary in modern systems.
B It should replace all other forms of justice.
C If it were built into institutions, trust would not have eroded.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — Mixed Conditionals

Grammar

Type 1: Past → Present (If + had + past participle, would + infinitive)

Use when an unreal past action has a present result.

Quando uma ação irreal no passado tem um resultado no presente.

IF Clause (Past)Main Clause (Present)
If the court had abolished the death penaltythe system would be more humane today.
If the legislature had upheld the reformcitizens would have greater protections now.

Type 2: Present → Past (If + past simple, would have + past participle)

Use when an unreal present situation would have changed the past.

Quando uma situação irreal no presente teria mudado o passado.

IF Clause (Present)Main Clause (Past)
If justice were truly impartialfewer wrongful convictions would have occurred.
If accountability were built into the systemfewer abuses would have gone unpunished.

Key difference from Third Conditional: Third conditional = past → past. Mixed conditionals MIX time frames (past↔present).

Diferença chave da Terceira Condicional: a terceira condicional é passado → passado. Condicionais mistas MISTURAM os tempos (passado↔presente).

Stage 1.5: Apply It

Practice
"If the court had abolished the death penalty, the system more humane today."
"If justice were truly impartial, fewer wrongful convictions ."
"If the legislature the reform, citizens would have greater protections now."
"If restorative justice the norm, many victims would have found closure years ago."
"If accountability were built into the system, fewer abuses unpunished."
"If the dissenting opinion , the law would protect more people today."

Stage 2: Put It in Order

Order

Arrange these sentences in the logical order of a debate about justice.

? "The question before us today is fundamental: what is the purpose of punishment?"
? "Retribution argues that wrongdoers deserve proportional consequences."
? "However, restorative justice focuses on repairing harm rather than inflicting more."
? "If equity were the foundation of every legal system, disproportionate sentencing would not exist."
? "Ultimately, justice must balance accountability with compassion."

Stage 3: Pronunciation Practice

Speaking
If the court had abolished the death penalty, the system would be more humane today.
If justice were truly impartial, fewer wrongful convictions would have occurred.
Accountability is essential for maintaining public trust in the legal system.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
You are debating a colleague who believes punishment should only be retributive. How do you introduce the concept of restorative justice?
A "We should just let everyone go free."
B "If restorative justice were the norm, many victims would have found closure years ago."
C "Punishment is always wrong."
A journalist asks why wrongful convictions persist. Using a mixed conditional, how do you respond?
A "Because judges are incompetent."
B "If we tried harder, things would improve."
C "If accountability were built into the system, fewer abuses would have gone unpunished."
At a conference, someone argues that certain rights cannot be removed by any government. Which term best describes this?
A Proportional
B Inalienable
C Disproportionate
You want to express that a past decision by the legislature affects the present. Which mixed conditional is correct?
A "If the legislature had upheld the reform, citizens would have greater protections now."
B "If the legislature upholds the reform, citizens will have greater protections."
C "If the legislature had upheld the reform, citizens would have had protections."

Stage 5: Reflection

Reflection
Using mixed conditionals, reflect on the justice system in Brazil. Choose one of these prompts:

1. "If the Brazilian justice system had prioritized equity from the start, how would things be different today?"
2. "If restorative justice were the norm in Brazil, what past outcomes would have been different?"

Record your response (aim for at least 30 seconds).

Suggested response:

"If the Brazilian justice system had prioritized equity from the very beginning, marginalized communities would have better access to legal representation today. If restorative justice were the norm, many nonviolent offenders would have been rehabilitated rather than incarcerated. If accountability were truly fundamental to our institutions, disproportionate sentencing would never have become so widespread."

Survival Card — Lesson 6

1If the court had prioritized equity, disproportionate sentencing would not be so widespread today.Se o tribunal tivesse priorizado a equidade, sentenças desproporcionais não seriam tão difundidas hoje.
2If accountability were built into the system from the start, public trust would be stronger now.Se a responsabilização fosse construída no sistema desde o início, a confiança pública seria mais forte agora.
3If the dissenting opinion had prevailed, the law would protect more people today.Se a opinião dissidente tivesse prevalecido, a lei protegeria mais pessoas hoje.
4If restorative justice had been adopted earlier, many communities would have healed instead of fractured.Se a justiça restaurativa tivesse sido adotada antes, muitas comunidades teriam se curado em vez de se fraturar.
5If we truly believed that rights were inalienable, we would never have tolerated such inequity.Se realmente acreditássemos que os direitos são inalienáveis, nunca teríamos tolerado tal desigualdade.
Aula 07 — Pre-class

The Expert Witness — Giving Detailed Accounts

Narrative Tenses Review (Past Simple, Past Continuous, Past Perfect) · Courtroom testimony vocabulary
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary
Witness·A person who sees an event and can describe it in court.
"The witness identified the suspect from a lineup of six individuals."
Testemunha
Testify·To give evidence or a statement under oath.
"The forensic expert was called to testify about the DNA results."
Testemunhar / Depor
Account·A description or report of events.
"The victim gave a detailed account of the robbery to the police."
Relato
Sequence·The order in which events occurred.
"The detective reconstructed the sequence of events before the crime."
Sequência
Recollect·To remember something from the past.
"The witness could not recollect the exact time of the incident."
Recordar / Lembrar
Corroborate·To confirm or support a statement with evidence.
"Security footage corroborated the witness's account of the attack."
Corroborar
Discrepancy·A difference between two things that should be the same.
"The defense found a discrepancy between the two witness statements."
Discrepância
Sworn·Made under a formal oath; legally binding.
"The officer submitted a sworn statement describing the arrest."
Juramentado
Reliable·Consistently trustworthy and accurate.
"The court must determine whether the eyewitness testimony is reliable."
Confiável
Chronological·Arranged in the order of time.
"The prosecutor presented the evidence in chronological order."
Cronológico
Recount·To tell someone about events in detail; narrate.
"The victim was asked to recount exactly what happened that night."
Relatar / Narrar
Deposition·Formal sworn testimony given outside of court.
"The attorney took the witness's deposition three weeks before the trial."
Deposição / Depoimento

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice
Witness
Testify
Sequence
Corroborate
Discrepancy
Chronological
Deposition

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Read the passage and notice the narrative tenses in bold.

On the night of March fifteenth, a disturbance occurred near a bank on Avenida Paulista. A witness who was walking home from work heard shouting and stopped to observe. While he was standing on the opposite sidewalk, he noticed a man running from the building carrying a dark bag.

The witness had already called the police before the suspect fled the scene. When officers arrived, they found the witness exactly where the security camera later corroborated his position. The detective took a sworn statement that night and later discovered that the witness's account was entirely reliable.

The defense attorney had been reviewing the deposition for weeks before the trial began. He found no discrepancy between his client's chronological recount and the physical evidence. The forensic team was collecting samples when the rain started, but they had secured the critical evidence in time.

1. Which tense describes the background action "was walking home"?
A Past Simple
B Past Continuous
C Past Perfect
2. Why is "had already called" in the Past Perfect?
A It happened at the same time as the suspect fleeing.
B It happened before another past action (the suspect fleeing).
C It describes a habitual past action.
3. In "The forensic team was collecting samples when the rain started," what role does Past Continuous play?
A It sets the background action that was interrupted.
B It describes a completed action.
C It shows an action that happened before the rain.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — Narrative Tenses

Grammar

Past Simple — Completed actions in sequence

Use for main events that happened one after another.

StructureExample
Subject + past verbThe witness identified the suspect.
Subject + did not + baseThe officer did not find any evidence.

Past Continuous — Background / interrupted actions

Use for actions in progress when something else happened.

StructureExample
Subject + was/were + -ingThe witness was walking home when he heard the noise.
While + past continuous, past simpleWhile the jury was deliberating, the judge reviewed the case.

Past Perfect — Earlier past (before another past event)

Use for actions completed before the main narrative moment.

StructureExample
Subject + had + past participleShe had already left when the police arrived.
By the time + past simple, past perfectBy the time the trial began, the team had gathered all the evidence.

Key pattern: Past Simple = main events. Past Continuous = background. Past Perfect = earlier past. Combine all three to build a clear, detailed narrative.

Stage 1.5: Apply It

Practice
"While the jury , the judge reviewed the sworn statements."
"The detective three witnesses before he found the key evidence."
"The suspect the building when the alarm went off."
"The witness recounted that she the defendant near the crime scene at eight pm."
"The forensic team evidence when the rain started to fall."
"By the time the trial began, the prosecution over fifty pieces of evidence."

Stage 2: Put It in Order

Order

Arrange these sentences in the chronological order of a witness statement.

? "The witness had finished his shift at eight thirty pm and was walking toward the metro station."
? "While he was crossing Avenida Paulista, he heard shouting near the bank."
? "He stopped on the opposite sidewalk and noticed a man running from the building."
? "He had already called the police by the time the suspect fled the scene."
? "When the officers arrived, they found the witness standing exactly where the camera confirmed his position."

Stage 3: Pronunciation Practice

Speaking
The witness was walking home when he heard a loud noise coming from the building.
She had already left the office when the police arrived at the scene.
The defendant entered the building while the security guard was checking the cameras.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
A judge asks you to describe the sequence of events on the night of the crime. Which sentence correctly combines narrative tenses?
A "My client walks home when he was hearing a noise."
B "My client was walking home when he heard a loud noise."
C "My client had walked home when he was hearing a noise."
You need to explain that the police arrived after your client had already called them. Which is correct?
A "My client had already called the police before the officers arrived."
B "My client already called the police before the officers arrived."
C "My client was already calling the police before the officers were arriving."
A prosecution witness says there is a discrepancy in the timeline. You want to describe two simultaneous actions. Which is best?
A "While the detective interviewed witnesses, the forensic team collected evidence."
B "While the detective was interviewing witnesses, the forensic team was collecting evidence."
C "While the detective had interviewed witnesses, the forensic team had collected evidence."
You are recounting what a witness told the court about events that happened before the crime. Which structure is correct?
A "The witness testified that the defendant threatened the victim."
B "The witness testified that the defendant had threatened the victim before the incident."
C "The witness testified that the defendant was threatening the victim before the incident."

Stage 5: Reflection

Reflection
Imagine you are preparing a witness statement for court. Using narrative tenses, describe a memorable case you have worked on. Include:

1. What was happening when the key event occurred (Past Continuous)
2. What had happened before that moment (Past Perfect)
3. What happened next (Past Simple)

Record your response (aim for at least 45 seconds).

Suggested response:

"I was working on a complex robbery case last year. The defendant had been cooperating with the investigation for several weeks when new evidence emerged. A witness who had not testified before came forward and recounted what she had seen on the night of the crime. She was walking near the scene when she noticed two men arguing. She had already called the police by the time one of them fled. Her sworn account corroborated my client's version of events, and the court found no discrepancy between her deposition and the security footage."

Survival Card — Lesson 7

1The witness was walking home when he heard a loud noise.A testemunha estava caminhando para casa quando ouviu um barulho alto.
2My client had already called the police before the suspect fled.Meu cliente já havia ligado para a polícia antes de o suspeito fugir.
3While the officers were securing the area, the detective was interviewing witnesses.Enquanto os policiais isolavam a área, o detetive entrevistava as testemunhas.
4The forensic team had collected all the evidence by the time the trial began.A equipe forense já havia coletado todas as provas quando o julgamento começou.
5I can corroborate my client's account with the security footage.Posso corroborar o relato do meu cliente com as imagens de segurança.
Aula 08 — Pre-class

Block 1 Review & Assessment

All Block 1 Structures · Comprehensive vocabulary and grammar review
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Review

Vocabulary
Precedent·A previous case used as a standard for future decisions.
"The ruling set a precedent that changed how courts handle similar cases."
Precedente
Cross-examination·Questioning a witness by the opposing attorney.
"The defense attorney's cross-examination revealed several inconsistencies."
Contrainterrogatório
Allegation·A claim that someone has done something illegal.
"The allegations against the official were never proven in court."
Alegação
Custody·Being held by police; legal right to care for someone.
"The suspect was taken into custody pending a bail hearing."
Custódia
Tort·A civil wrong that causes harm to another person.
"The plaintiff filed a tort claim for damages caused by negligence."
Ilícito civil
Sovereignty·The authority of a state to govern itself.
"Each nation's sovereignty must be respected in international agreements."
Soberania
Deterrent·Something that discourages a particular action.
"Mandatory minimum sentences are intended to serve as a deterrent."
Dissuasor / Inibidor
Compliance·The act of following rules and regulations.
"The firm was fined for failure to maintain regulatory compliance."
Conformidade
Perjury·The crime of lying under oath in court.
"The witness was charged with perjury after contradicting her sworn statement."
Perjúrio
Restitution·Compensation ordered by the court for losses.
"The court ordered full restitution to the victims of the fraud."
Restituição
Accountability·Being responsible for one's actions and decisions.
"Accountability within the judiciary is essential for maintaining public trust."
Responsabilização
Deposition·A formal witness statement taken outside of court.
"The attorney took the witness's deposition two weeks before the trial."
Deposição / Depoimento formal

Stage 1.2: Matching Review

Practice
Precedent
Cross-examination
Allegation
Custody
Tort
Sovereignty
Deterrent
Compliance
Perjury
Restitution
Accountability
Deposition

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Throughout my career, I have handled cases involving precedent-setting rulings, and I have been advocating for greater accountability in the system. A judge who fails to ensure compliance with due process undermines the sovereignty of the legal system. If the court imposed harsher penalties for perjury, witnesses would think twice before lying under oath. If the prosecution had secured the deposition earlier, the case would not have been dismissed. If accountability were built into every institution, fewer wrongful convictions would have occurred. The witness was giving her deposition when the defense attorney interrupted with a cross-examination request.

Which grammar structure appears in "I have handled cases"?
A Past Simple
B Present Perfect Simple
C Past Perfect
What type of conditional is "If the court imposed harsher penalties, witnesses would think twice"?
A Third Conditional
B Second Conditional
C Mixed Conditional
In "If accountability were built into every institution, fewer wrongful convictions would have occurred," what type of conditional is this?
A Second Conditional
B Third Conditional
C Mixed Conditional
"The witness was giving her deposition when the attorney interrupted" uses which tenses?
A Past Continuous + Past Simple
B Past Perfect + Past Simple
C Present Perfect + Past Simple

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip — Block 1 Review

Grammar

Block 1 Grammar Summary

1. Present Perfect Simple (have/has + past participle) — completed actions with present relevance.

Ações concluídas com relevância no presente.

I have handled hundreds of cases.

2. Present Perfect Continuous (have/has + been + -ing) — ongoing actions emphasizing duration.

Ações em andamento com ênfase na duração.

I have been working on this case for three months.

3. Relative Clauses — defining (who/which/that, no commas) vs non-defining (who/which, with commas).

Restritivas (sem vírgula) vs explicativas (com vírgula).

Defining: Judges who serve on the Supreme Court interpret the constitution.
Non-defining: Arbitration, which is growing in popularity, resolves disputes outside court.

4. Second Conditional (If + past simple, would + infinitive) — hypothetical present/future.

Situações hipotéticas no presente/futuro.

If the court imposed harsher penalties, witnesses would think twice.

5. Third Conditional (If + had + past participle, would have + past participle) — imaginary past.

Passado imaginário — o evento NÃO aconteceu.

If the prosecution had secured the deposition, the case would not have been dismissed.

6. Mixed Conditionals — combine different time frames (past → present or present → past).

Combinam tempos diferentes (passado → presente ou presente → passado).

If accountability were built into every institution, fewer wrongful convictions would have occurred.

7. Narrative Tenses (Past Simple + Past Continuous + Past Perfect) — telling stories and recounting events.

Narrando histórias e relatando eventos.

The witness was giving her deposition when the attorney interrupted. She had already signed the statement.

Stage 1.5: Application — Fill in the Blank

Practice
"I on precedent-setting cases for over twenty years."
"The investigation, uncovered multiple allegations, led to a landmark tort case."
"If the state restitution sooner, the victims would not be suffering today."
"If compliance mandatory for all institutions, fewer cases of negligence would occur."
"The detective the deposition before the suspect was taken into custody."
"She the allegations when the judge called for a recess."

Stage 2: Sentence Ordering

Order

Listen to the sentence, then arrange the words in the correct order.

? The ruling
? allegations of perjury.
? for how courts
? set a precedent
? handle

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking
Cross-examination is a fundamental tool that every defense attorney must master.
O contrainterrogatório é uma ferramenta fundamental que todo advogado de defesa deve dominar.
The court ordered restitution because the defendant had caused significant financial harm.
O tribunal ordenou restituição porque o réu havia causado prejuízo financeiro significativo.
If sovereignty were not protected by international law, smaller nations would be vulnerable to exploitation.
Se a soberania não fosse protegida pelo direito internacional, nações menores seriam vulneráveis à exploração.
The witness had given a sworn deposition, but during cross-examination, she contradicted her earlier account.
A testemunha havia prestado um depoimento juramentado, mas durante o contrainterrogatório, ela contradisse seu relato anterior.
If the deterrent had been stronger, the crime rate would have decreased.
Se o efeito dissuasor tivesse sido mais forte, a taxa de criminalidade teria diminuído.

Stage 4: Grammar Identification Quiz

Quiz
"I have been advocating for reform for years." — Identify the structure:
A Present Perfect Simple
B Present Perfect Continuous
C Past Continuous
"If the court had ensured compliance, the tort claim would never have been filed." — This is:
A Second Conditional
B Third Conditional
C Mixed Conditional
"Arbitration, which is an alternative to litigation, resolves disputes efficiently." — The relative clause is:
A Defining (restrictive)
B Non-defining (explanatory)
C Reduced relative clause
"If restitution were always proportional, the system would be more equitable." — This is:
A Second Conditional
B Third Conditional
C Mixed Conditional
"The suspect was fleeing when the officers arrived. He had already destroyed the evidence." — The tenses used are:
A Past Continuous, Past Simple, Past Perfect
B Past Simple, Past Simple, Present Perfect
C Past Perfect, Past Simple, Past Continuous

Stage 5: Free Production — Conference Preparation

Reflection
Imagine you are preparing a 2-minute speech for an international legal conference. In your speech, you must: (1) introduce yourself using Present Perfect, (2) describe a past case using narrative tenses, (3) discuss a hypothetical improvement to the justice system using a conditional, and (4) use at least 4 vocabulary words from this block. Record yourself delivering the speech.

What I Learned — Lesson 8

Survival Card — Lesson 8

1Throughout my career, I have handled cases that set important precedents for accountability in the justice system.Ao longo da minha carreira, lidei com casos que estabeleceram precedentes importantes para a responsabilização no sistema de justiça.
2If compliance with due process had been ensured, the tort claim would never have been filed.Se a conformidade com o devido processo legal tivesse sido garantida, a ação por ilícito civil nunca teria sido ajuizada.
3The witness was taken into custody after the court discovered she had committed perjury.A testemunha foi levada sob custódia após o tribunal descobrir que ela havia cometido perjúrio.
4If restitution were always proportional to the harm caused, the system would be more equitable.Se a restituição fosse sempre proporcional ao dano causado, o sistema seria mais equitativo.
5The deposition, which corroborated the allegations, proved to be the turning point of the case.O depoimento, que corroborou as alegações, provou ser o ponto de virada do caso.

Block Complete!

You have completed all exercises. Well done!
