Chapter 1 — The Invitation
The Weight of Words
Diagnostic + Complex Professional Identity
Present Perfect Simple vs Present Perfect Continuous
Chapter 1 — The Invitation
Warm-up Discussion
Have you ever attended an international legal conference? What was the experience like?
What topics in criminal law have you been most interested in recently?
If you could present at a symposium abroad, what would you talk about?
Chapter 1 — The Invitation
The Scenario

You have received an invitation to the International Legal Symposium in Washington, D.C.

You will meet attorneys, prosecutors, and legal scholars from around the world. You need to introduce yourself, discuss your experience, and participate in panel discussions about criminal justice.

Today, we prepare for that moment.

Chapter 2 — Packing Words
Legal Vocabulary
13 essential words for your symposium toolkit
Chapter 2 — Packing Words
Click to reveal each word
A formal declaration of innocence
Acquittal
A formal declaration that someone is not guilty
"The jury returned a verdict of acquittal."
The obligation to prove allegations
Burden of proof
The duty to prove what is alleged in court
"The burden of proof lies with the prosecution."
Questioning a witness from the other side
Cross-examination
Questioning a witness by the opposing attorney
"The defense attorney challenged the witness during cross-examination."
Fair treatment through the legal system
Due process
The legal right to fair treatment in judicial proceedings
"Every defendant has a right to due process."
0 / 4 words revealed
Chapter 2 — Packing Words
More essential terms
The authority to make legal decisions
Jurisdiction
The official power to make legal decisions
"The court did not have jurisdiction over the case."
The process of taking legal action
Litigation
The process of resolving disputes through lawsuits
"Complex litigation can take years to resolve."
To reverse a legal decision
Overturn
To reverse a previous legal decision on appeal
"The appeals court decided to overturn the ruling."
An agreement for a lesser charge
Plea bargain
A deal where the defendant pleads guilty for a lighter sentence
"The defendant accepted a plea bargain."
0 / 4 words revealed
Chapter 2 — Packing Words
Five more key terms
Precedent A previous case that serves as a rule for future decisions
Ruling An official decision made by a judge or court
Testimony A formal statement given under oath in court
Verdict The decision made by a jury — guilty or not guilty
Advocate To publicly support a cause; a person who pleads for another
Chapter 2 — Packing Words
Vocabulary Check
What is the difference between a ruling and a verdict?
A ruling is a decision made by a judge; a verdict is a decision made by a jury.
Can you explain what "due process" means in your own words?
Due process means the government must follow fair legal procedures and respect all legal rights.
When might a defendant consider accepting a plea bargain?
When the evidence is strong against them and they want to receive a lighter sentence.
Chapter 3 — The Code
Grammar Discovery
Present Perfect Simple vs Present Perfect Continuous
Chapter 3 — The Code
Look at these sentences. What pattern do you see?
I have handled hundreds of cases. (result/quantity)
I have been working as a defender for twenty years. (duration/process)
The court has already issued its ruling. (completed action)
The jury has been deliberating since yesterday. (ongoing process)

What do the highlighted words have in common?

Chapter 3 — The Code
Discovery Question

When do we use have + past participle and when do we use have been + -ing?

FormStructureUse
Present Perfect Simplehave/has + past participleCompleted actions, results, quantity, experience
Present Perfect Continuoushave/has been + -ingOngoing actions, duration, process, recent activity
Chapter 3 — The Code
Common Mistake
"I have been handling 300 cases in my career."
"I have handled 300 cases in my career."

Use Simple for completed quantities. Use Continuous for duration and ongoing processes.

Chapter 3 — The Code
Grammar Practice — Click to reveal
The defense attorney ___has been preparing the cross-examination all week. (ongoing process)
The prosecution ___has already presented its closing argument. (completed action)
I ___have worked on this type of case before. (experience)
They ___have been negotiating a settlement since last month. (duration)
Chapter 4 — First Contact
The Defender's Dilemma
Luiz meets Mark Thompson at the International Legal Symposium
Chapter 4 — First Contact
Dialogue — At the Symposium
M
Good afternoon. Welcome to the International Legal Symposium. I am Mark Thompson, a criminal defense attorney from Washington, D.C.
L
Nice to meet you. I am Luiz Bressane, a public defender from São Paulo.
M
How long have you been practicing criminal law?
L
I have been working in criminal defense for over twenty years now.
M
That is impressive. Have you handled many jury trials?
L
Yes, I have handled hundreds of cases, including complex homicide trials.
M
In the United States, we have been seeing a shift toward plea bargains.
L
That is interesting. In Brazil, we have been debating plea bargains as well.
M
Have you ever had a case where the verdict was overturned on appeal?
L
Yes, I have. The appeals court overturned a ruling last year due to a due process violation.
M
That must have been a significant precedent.
L
It was. It has changed how we approach cross-examination in similar cases.
Chapter 4 — First Contact
Dialogue Comprehension
How long has Luiz been working in criminal defense?
Over twenty years.
What trend has Mark noticed in the United States?
A shift toward plea bargains.
Why was the ruling overturned in Luiz's case?
Due to a due process violation.
Chapter 4 — First Contact
Listening 1 — Luiz's Symposium Speech

Listen carefully. Answer the questions after the audio.

0:000:00
Speed
What type of cases has Luiz been focusing on recently?
Cases involving due process violations and precedent-setting rulings.
What has Luiz been advocating for?
Reforms in the plea bargain system in Brazil.
Why has he been attending international symposiums?
To exchange ideas with colleagues from around the world.
Chapter 4 — First Contact
Listening 2 — Sarah Chen, Federal Prosecutor

Listen to another legal professional. How does her experience compare?

0:000:00
Speed
What area of law does Sarah specialize in?
White-collar crime — corporate fraud, insider trading, money laundering.
What has she noticed about cross-examination techniques?
They have evolved significantly, with more technology being used.
What precedent has her landmark case set?
A new precedent for how courts handle digital testimony.
Chapter 5 — Practice
Sharpen Your Skills
Quick Fire + Spot the Error + Drilling
Chapter 5 — Practice
Quick Fire
0 / 6
Question 1 of 6
An attorney at the symposium asks: "Tell me about your career." Introduce yourself using Present Perfect.
"I have been working as a public defender for over twenty years. I have handled hundreds of criminal cases, including complex homicide trials."
Chapter 5 — Practice
Spot the Error
"I am working here since 2004."
"I have been working here since 2004."
"The jury has been reaching a verdict yesterday."
"The jury reached a verdict yesterday." (Past Simple for specific past time)
"I have been handling 300 cases in my career."
"I have handled 300 cases in my career." (Simple for quantity)
"She has testified three times since she has started working here."
"She has testified three times since she started working here." (since + Past Simple)
"We have been already reviewed the evidence."
"We have already reviewed the evidence." (Simple for completed + adverb placement)
0 / 5 errors found
Chapter 5 — Practice
Pronunciation Drilling
The defense attorney has been preparing the cross-examination all week.
The prosecution has already presented its closing argument.
I have worked on this type of case before.
They have been negotiating a settlement since last month.
Chapter 5 — Practice
Compare the Two Professionals
What do Luiz and Sarah have in common?
Both work in criminal law, have extensive experience, and have been dealing with important cases.
How do their specializations differ?
Luiz focuses on criminal defense (homicide trials); Sarah focuses on white-collar crime (fraud, insider trading).
What innovation has Sarah mentioned?
The use of technology in the courtroom, including digital evidence and digital testimony.
Chapter 6 — Your Turn
Role-Play
Guided → Semi-free → Free Production
Chapter 6 — Your Turn
Guided Role-Play
You are at the symposium welcome reception. An American attorney approaches you and says: "Hi, I'm Mark. I've been working in federal court for 15 years. What about you?" Introduce yourself and discuss your experience.
have been working have handled public defender criminal cases homicide trials twenty years
Chapter 6 — Your Turn
Semi-Free Role-Play
You are on a panel discussing "Criminal Justice Reform." The moderator asks you to share how the Brazilian system has been evolving. Discuss your experience with plea bargains, due process, and recent precedents.
have been debating precedent overturned due process plea bargain
Chapter 6 — Your Turn
Free Production
After the panel, a journalist approaches you and says: "I've been covering legal reform in Latin America. Can you tell me about the most significant case you have handled and what impact it has had?" Speak freely for 2-3 minutes.
No keywords — speak freely!
Chapter 7 — The Briefcase
Wrap-Up
Survival phrases, checklist, and your badge
Chapter 7 — The Briefcase
Survival Card — Lesson 1
1
I have been practicing law for over twenty years.
2
The prosecution has not met the burden of proof in this case.
3
We have been reviewing the testimony for inconsistencies.
4
The court has set an important precedent with this ruling.
5
I would argue that the evidence does not support the verdict.
Chapter 7 — The Briefcase
What I Learned Today
I can use Present Perfect Simple for results and completed actions.
I can use Present Perfect Continuous for ongoing processes and duration.
I can introduce myself at a legal symposium in English.
I know 13 legal terms: acquittal, burden of proof, cross-examination, etc.
I can discuss my professional experience with precision and fluency.
Badge Earned!

The Weight of Words — Lesson 1 Complete

Chapter 7 — The Briefcase
Before Next Class

1. Write a 150-word symposium self-introduction

2. Use at least 5 vocabulary words from today

3. Include both Present Perfect Simple and Continuous

4. Complete the Pre-class exercises for Lesson 2

Chapter 7 — The Briefcase
Final Reflection
Record a 60-second reflection: What was the most important thing you learned today? What would you like to practice more?
Coming Next
Lesson 2 — Building the Case

Describing Professional Experience · Mixed Tenses (Past + Present Perfect)

Lesson 1 — Complete
The Weight of Words

General + Legal English — B2 Track

Chapter 1 — The Interview Begins
Building the Case
Describing Professional Experience
Past Simple vs Present Perfect
Chapter 1 — The Interview Begins
Warm-up Discussion
Tell me about how you became interested in law. When did it start?
What is the most important case you have worked on? What happened?
If a journalist interviewed you about your career, what would you want to highlight?
Chapter 1 — The Interview Begins
The Scenario

At the symposium, you meet Sarah Chen, a journalist from The Atlantic.

She is writing a profile on international public defenders and wants to interview you. She will ask about your career journey — when you started, key cases, and what you have accomplished.

Today, you tell your story with precision.

Chapter 2 — Packing Words
Legal Vocabulary II
13 new words for describing your career
Chapter 2 — Packing Words
Click to reveal each word
To bring legal action against someone
Prosecute
To conduct legal proceedings against someone
"The district attorney decided to prosecute the suspect."
The punishment given after conviction
Sentence
The punishment assigned by a court after conviction
"The judge handed down a sentence of fifteen years."
Asking a higher court to review
Appeal
A formal request for a higher court to review a decision
"The defense filed an appeal after the guilty verdict."
To reject or throw out a case
Dismiss
To reject or throw out a legal case
"The judge dismissed the case due to lack of evidence."
0 / 4 words revealed
Chapter 2 — Packing Words
More essential terms
A person who testifies under oath
Witness
A person who testifies under oath in court
"The key witness testified that she saw the defendant."
Being held by authorities
Custody
The state of being detained by authorities
"The suspect was taken into custody."
Temporary release for a financial guarantee
Bail
Temporary release from custody in exchange for a guarantee
"The defendant posted bail and was released."
A formal written law
Statute
A formal written law enacted by a legislature
"The new statute came into effect last January."
0 / 4 words revealed
Chapter 2 — Packing Words
Five more key terms
Allegation An unproven claim that someone has committed a wrongful act
Indict To formally charge someone with a serious crime (the "c" is silent: in-DITE)
Convict (v.) To declare someone guilty of a criminal offense
Circumstantial Based on indirect evidence rather than direct proof
Mitigate To make something less severe or serious
Chapter 3 — The Code
Grammar Discovery
Past Simple vs Present Perfect
Chapter 3 — The Code
Look at these pairs. What pattern do you see?
I started my career in 2003. (specific time)
I have prosecuted over two hundred cases. (no specific time)
Last year, the court dismissed three of my cases. (specific time)
I have never lost an appeal at the federal level. (experience up to now)

When do we use Past Simple and when do we use Present Perfect?

Chapter 3 — The Code
Discovery Question

One tense tells us WHEN. The other tells us WHAT (connected to now).

TenseStructureUseSignal words
Past Simpleverb + -ed / irregularCompleted actions at a specific timein 2005, last year, yesterday, when
Present Perfecthave/has + past participleExperiences/achievements connected to nowever, never, already, yet, since, throughout
Chapter 3 — The Code
Common Mistake
"I have started my career in 2003."
"I started my career in 2003."
"I prosecuted many cases throughout my career."
"I have prosecuted many cases throughout my career."

Specific time? Past Simple. Connected to now? Present Perfect.

Chapter 3 — The Code
Grammar Practice — Click to reveal
I ___prosecuted my first case in 2005. (specific year)
I ___have handled over two hundred cases since then. (since = no specific time)
The witness ___testified last week. (specific time)
The jury ___has not reached a verdict yet. (yet = connected to now)
Chapter 4 — The Story Unfolds
The Interview
Sarah Chen interviews Luiz Bressane for The Atlantic
Chapter 4 — The Story Unfolds
Dialogue — The Interview
S
Mr. Bressane, thank you for agreeing to this interview. I am Sarah Chen, a journalist with The Atlantic.
L
It is my pleasure, Ms. Chen. I have been looking forward to sharing my perspective.
S
When did you first become interested in criminal defense?
L
I studied law at the University of São Paulo and graduated in 2002. I knew from my first year that I wanted to defend people who could not afford a lawyer.
S
What was your first major case?
L
In 2005, I defended a man who was indicted for a crime he did not commit. The evidence was entirely circumstantial. We mitigated the charges, and eventually the case was dismissed.
S
That must have been a defining moment. How has that experience shaped your career?
L
It taught me that the justice system is imperfect. Since then, I have defended hundreds of clients. I have also worked to reform bail statutes in my state.
S
Have you ever had a case where the sentence seemed unjust?
L
Yes, several times. Last year, a client was convicted based on a single witness testimony. We filed an appeal, and the higher court reduced the sentence significantly.
Chapter 4 — The Story Unfolds
Dialogue Comprehension
When did Luiz graduate from law school?
In 2002, from the University of São Paulo.
What happened with his first major case in 2005?
He defended a man wrongfully indicted. The evidence was circumstantial, and the case was dismissed.
What happened last year with the unjust sentence?
A client was convicted on a single witness testimony. Luiz filed an appeal and the sentence was reduced.
Chapter 4 — The Story Unfolds
Listening 1 — Luiz's Career Path

Listen carefully. Answer the questions after the audio.

0:000:00
Speed
What did Luiz handle in his first year as a public defender?
Minor custody disputes and bail hearings.
What landmark case did he take on in 2005?
A wrongful indictment where the evidence was circumstantial and the charges were dismissed.
What has he never done at the federal level?
He has never lost an appeal.
Chapter 4 — The Story Unfolds
Listening 2 — Three Professionals, Three Stories

Listen to a news report about legal professionals. How do their experiences compare?

0:000:00
Speed
What did Maria Santos prosecute in 2010?
A high-profile corruption case that lasted four years. The defendant was convicted on all counts.
Why did Judge Rivera dismiss the indictment in 2015?
New witness testimony emerged, and the allegations were dropped.
What has Professor Vasquez's research helped to mitigate?
Sentencing disparities in several European countries.
Chapter 4 — The Story Unfolds
Tense Analysis
"Maria Santos prosecuted a corruption case in 2010." Which tense and why?
Past Simple — specific year (2010).
"Her research has helped mitigate sentencing disparities." Which tense and why?
Present Perfect — achievement connected to the present, no specific time.
"Judge Rivera dismissed a controversial indictment in 2015." Which tense and why?
Past Simple — specific year (2015).
Chapter 4 — The Story Unfolds
Interview Practice — Click to reveal
The prosecution ___prosecuted the case aggressively last year. (last year = specific)
After the witness changed her testimony, the judge ___dismissed the indictment. (sequence of past events)
I ___have never lost an appeal at the federal level. (never = experience up to now)
The defendant ___received a reduced sentence because the evidence was circumstantial. (completed past event)
Chapter 4 — The Story Unfolds
Shadowing — Listen and Repeat
I prosecuted my first case in 2005, and I have handled over two hundred since then.
The witness testified last week, but the jury has not reached a verdict yet.
The judge dismissed the indictment in March, and no one has filed an appeal since.
I graduated from law school in 2002, and I have been practicing ever since.
Chapter 5 — Practice
Sharpen Your Skills
Quick Fire + Spot the Error + Drilling
Chapter 5 — Practice
Quick Fire
0 / 6
Question 1 of 6
Sarah asks: "When did you become a public defender?" Describe when and where you started.
"I became a public defender in 2003. I started working at the São Paulo Public Defender's Office right after passing the bar exam."
Chapter 5 — Practice
Spot the Error
"I have graduated from law school in 2002."
"I graduated from law school in 2002." (specific year = Past Simple)
"The defendant posted bail and already left the country."
"The defendant posted bail and has already left the country." (already = Present Perfect)
"Last month, the judge has dismissed three cases."
"Last month, the judge dismissed three cases." (last month = Past Simple)
"I never lost an appeal at the federal level."
"I have never lost an appeal at the federal level." (never = Present Perfect)
"She has testified in court yesterday."
"She testified in court yesterday." (yesterday = Past Simple)
0 / 5 errors found
Chapter 5 — Practice
Mixed Tense Drilling
I prosecuted my first case in 2005, and I have handled over two hundred since then.
The witness testified last week, but the jury has not reached a verdict yet.
The judge dismissed the indictment in March, and no one has filed an appeal since.
Chapter 5 — Practice
Compare the Three Professionals
What did Maria Santos prosecute in 2010? What was the result?
She prosecuted a high-profile corruption case. The defendant was convicted on all counts.
What happened when new witness testimony emerged in Judge Rivera's case?
He dismissed the indictment, and the suspect was released from custody.
What has Professor Vasquez's research accomplished?
It has helped mitigate sentencing disparities in several European countries.
Chapter 6 — Your Turn
The Profile
Guided → Semi-free → Free Production
Chapter 6 — Your Turn
Guided Role-Play
Sarah Chen asks: "Tell me about how you became a public defender. When did you start? What have you accomplished since then?" Answer using both past simple and present perfect.
started / graduated have defended have never lost indicted / dismissed custody / bail
Chapter 6 — Your Turn
Semi-Free Role-Play
Sarah says: "Tell me about a specific case that changed your perspective, and then tell me what you have accomplished since that moment." You must use Past Simple for the specific case and Present Perfect for your achievements.
convicted / acquitted witness / testimony have worked / have reformed circumstantial mitigate
Chapter 6 — Your Turn
Free Production
Sarah's final question: "Looking at your entire career — the cases, the reforms, the challenges — what would you like readers of The Atlantic to understand about the Brazilian justice system?" Speak freely for 2-3 minutes.
No keywords — speak freely!
Chapter 7 — The Profile
Wrap-Up
Survival phrases, checklist, and your badge
Chapter 7 — The Profile
Survival Card — Lesson 2
1
The defendant posted bail yesterday and has already left the country.
2
The grand jury indicted the suspect, but the case was dismissed last month.
3
I have defended clients in over fifty custody hearings throughout my career.
4
The witness changed her testimony, and the judge reduced the sentence.
5
Circumstantial evidence alone has never been enough to convict in my experience.
Chapter 7 — The Profile
What I Learned Today
I can use Past Simple for completed actions at a specific time.
I can use Present Perfect for experiences and achievements connected to now.
I can describe my career timeline using mixed tenses correctly.
I know 13 new legal terms: prosecute, sentence, appeal, dismiss, witness, custody, bail, statute, allegation, indict, convict, circumstantial, mitigate.
I can answer interview questions about my professional experience fluently.
Badge Earned!

Building the Case — Lesson 2 Complete

Before Next Class

1. Write a 200-word career narrative for a journalist

2. Use at least 6 vocabulary words from today

Lesson 2 — Complete
Building the Case

General + Legal English — B2 Track

Chapter 1 — Two Worlds
The System
Comparing Legal Systems
Relative Clauses — Defining vs Non-defining
Chapter 1 — Two Worlds
Warm-up Discussion
What do you know about the differences between Brazilian and American law?
Have you ever worked on a case that involved comparing different legal systems?
If you could change one thing about the Brazilian legal system, what would it be?
Chapter 1 — Two Worlds
The Scenario

At the symposium, you have been invited to join a comparative law panel with Professor James Crawford, an American constitutional law professor.

Together, you will compare the Brazilian civil law system with the American common law system. You will discuss how courts, statutes, and legal principles differ between the two traditions.

Two worlds, one pursuit of justice.

Chapter 2 — Packing Words
Comparative Law Vocabulary
13 essential words for your panel discussion
Chapter 2 — Packing Words
Click to reveal each word
The person who brings a case to court
Plaintiff
A person who brings a case against another in court
"The plaintiff filed a lawsuit against the manufacturer."
The person accused or sued in court
Defendant
A person accused of a crime or sued in court
"The defendant denied all charges and entered a plea of not guilty."
The branch of government that interprets laws
Judiciary
The branch of government responsible for interpreting laws
"An independent judiciary is essential for a functioning democracy."
A wrongful act leading to civil liability
Tort
A wrongful act that causes harm and leads to civil liability
"A tort is a civil wrong that causes harm to another person."
0 / 4 words revealed
Chapter 2 — Packing Words
Click to reveal each word
Legally responsible for something
Liable
Legally responsible for something
"The company was found liable for the environmental damage."
Relating to or required by a statute
Statutory
Relating to or required by a statute or law
"The statutory age for voting is eighteen in most countries."
To make changes to a legal document
Amend
To make changes to a legal document or law
"Congress voted to amend the constitution to protect civil rights."
To hand over a person to another jurisdiction
Extradite
To hand over a person accused of a crime to another jurisdiction
"The government agreed to extradite the suspect to face trial abroad."
0 / 4 words revealed
Chapter 2 — Packing Words
Click to reveal each word
Resolving disputes outside of court
Arbitration
A method of resolving disputes outside of court
"The two companies resolved their dispute through arbitration."
To arrange laws into a systematic code
Codify
To arrange laws into a systematic code
"Napoleon was the first to codify French law into a unified system."
Law derived from custom and judicial decisions
Common law
Law derived from custom and judicial decisions rather than statutes
"Common law relies on judicial precedent rather than written statutes."
A legal order to bring a person before a judge
Habeas corpus
A legal order requiring a person to be brought before a judge
"The lawyer filed a habeas corpus petition to challenge the detention."
Supreme authority of a state to govern itself
Sovereignty
Supreme authority of a state to govern itself
"National sovereignty means a state has the right to govern itself."
0 / 5 words revealed
Chapter 3 — The Code
Relative Clauses
Defining vs Non-defining — Precision in Legal Language
Chapter 3 — The Code
Observe the Highlighted Words
Judges who serve on the Supreme Court interpret the constitution.
Countries that follow common law rely on judicial precedent.
Arbitration, which is growing in popularity, resolves disputes outside court.
Professor Crawford, who teaches at Georgetown, is an expert on comparative law.

What role do the highlighted words play?

Chapter 3 — The Code
The Rule

Defining (Restrictive)

Identifies WHICH person/thing
No commas
Can use: who, which, that

"Judges who serve on the Supreme Court..."

Non-defining (Non-restrictive)

Adds EXTRA information
With commas
Can use: who, which (NEVER that)

"Arbitration, which is growing,..."

Chapter 3 — The Code
Common Mistake
WRONG

"Arbitration, that is growing in popularity, resolves disputes."

CORRECT

"Arbitration, which is growing in popularity, resolves disputes."

Non-defining clauses (with commas) NEVER use "that" — always "who" or "which"

Chapter 3 — The Code
Practice — Complete with who, which, or that
Judges ___who serve on constitutional courts have the power to review legislation. (defining — people)
Arbitration, ___which is a popular alternative to litigation, helps resolve disputes. (non-defining — commas)
Countries ___that codify their laws follow the civil law tradition. (defining — things)
Habeas corpus, ___which protects individual freedom, exists in both traditions. (non-defining — commas)
A plaintiff ___who suffers harm can file a tort claim against the defendant. (defining — person)
Chapter 4 — The Panel
Two Worlds, One Justice
Professor Crawford & Luiz Compare Legal Systems
Chapter 4 — The Panel
Panel Discussion — Crawford & Luiz
C
Good afternoon, everyone. Welcome to the comparative law panel at the Washington symposium.
L
Thank you, Professor Crawford. It is an honor to be part of this discussion.
C
In the United States, which follows a common law tradition, judges rely heavily on precedent.
L
That is interesting. In Brazil, where the legal system is based on codified statutes, the role of the judiciary is different.
C
Can you explain how tort law works in Brazil?
L
Of course. In Brazilian civil law, a plaintiff who suffers harm can file a claim, and the defendant may be found liable under statutory provisions.
C
Here in the US, common law torts have evolved through judicial decisions rather than codified rules.
L
One area where our systems converge is arbitration, which has become increasingly popular in both countries.
C
Absolutely. And what about habeas corpus? How does it function in the Brazilian system?
L
Habeas corpus, which is guaranteed by our constitution, protects individuals from unlawful detention. It is a fundamental safeguard of sovereignty and individual rights.
Chapter 4 — The Panel
Dialogue Comprehension
What legal tradition does the United States follow?
Common law, which relies on judicial precedent.
How does tort law differ between the two systems?
In Brazil, liability is defined by statutory provisions. In the US, common law torts evolved through judicial decisions.
What role does habeas corpus play in Brazil?
It is guaranteed by the constitution and protects individuals from unlawful detention.
Chapter 4 — The Panel
Listening 1 — Comparative Law Lecture

Listen carefully. Answer the questions after the audio.

0:000:00
Speed
What is the main difference between common law and civil law?
Common law relies on judicial precedent; civil law is based on codified statutes.
How does tort liability differ between the two systems?
In common law, tort liability evolved through case law. In civil law, it is defined by statutory provisions.
Why might countries resist extraditing suspects?
Countries that value sovereignty often resist extraditing suspects to foreign jurisdictions.
Chapter 4 — The Panel
Listening 2 — Global Legal Reform

Listen to how different countries have reformed their legal systems.

0:000:00
Speed
What challenge does Professor Torres describe about codifying new statutes?
It requires balancing sovereignty with international obligations.
What tension exists in the Nigerian legal system?
Tension between customary law and statutory law, inherited from Britain's common law system.
What did attorney Tanaka find about arbitration in Japan?
Plaintiffs who choose arbitration resolve disputes faster, and defendants are more likely to accept settlements.
Chapter 4 — The Panel
Shadowing — Listen and Repeat
In common law systems, which originated in England, courts rely on judicial precedent.
A plaintiff who suffers harm can file a tort claim against the defendant.
Arbitration, which is growing in popularity, offers an alternative to traditional litigation.
Countries that codify their laws into comprehensive codes follow the civil law tradition.
Chapter 4 — The Panel
Identify: Defining or Non-defining?
"In the US, which follows a common law tradition, judges rely on precedent."
Non-defining — "which follows a common law tradition" adds extra info about the US (commas present).
"A plaintiff who suffers harm can file a claim."
Defining — "who suffers harm" restricts which plaintiff (no commas, essential meaning).
"Habeas corpus, which is guaranteed by the constitution, protects individuals."
Non-defining — "which is guaranteed by the constitution" adds extra info (commas, removable).
Chapter 4 — The Panel
Compare the Three Scholars
What has Professor Torres found about codifying new statutes?
She found that codifying new statutes requires balancing sovereignty with international obligations.
What tension does Dr. Okafor examine in Nigeria?
Nigeria, which inherited its legal system from Britain, faces tension between customary law and statutory law.
What advantage did attorney Tanaka find for arbitration?
Plaintiffs who choose arbitration resolve disputes faster than those who go through the courts.
Chapter 5 — Practice
Sharpen Your Skills
Quick Fire + Spot the Error + Drilling
Chapter 5 — Practice
Quick Fire
0 / 6
Question 1 of 6
An American colleague asks: "How does the Brazilian legal system work?" Explain using a relative clause.
"Brazil follows a civil law system, which is based on codified statutes. Judges who interpret the law focus on the written code rather than case precedent."
Chapter 5 — Practice
Spot the Error
"Arbitration, that is growing in popularity, resolves disputes."
"Arbitration, which is growing in popularity, resolves disputes." (Non-defining = never "that")
"The judiciary which interprets the law plays a crucial role."
"The judiciary, which interprets the law, plays a crucial role." (Non-defining needs commas — there is only one judiciary)
"Countries, that codify their laws, follow the civil law tradition."
"Countries that codify their laws follow the civil law tradition." (Defining = no commas, specifies which countries)
"Professor Crawford, that teaches at Georgetown, is an expert."
"Professor Crawford, who teaches at Georgetown, is an expert." (Non-defining + person = "who", never "that")
"A plaintiff, who suffers harm, can file a tort claim."
"A plaintiff who suffers harm can file a tort claim." (Defining = no commas, restricts which plaintiff)
0 / 5 errors found
Chapter 5 — Practice
Relative Clause Drilling
The judiciary, which interprets the law, plays a crucial role in both systems.
The government refused to extradite the suspect, citing national sovereignty.
Habeas corpus protects individuals who are unlawfully detained by the state.
Chapter 5 — Practice
Create Sentences with Relative Clauses
Topic: Arbitration + growing in popularity (non-defining)
"Arbitration, which is growing in popularity, offers an efficient alternative to going to court."
Topic: Judges + interpret the constitution (defining)
"Judges who interpret the constitution have significant power in shaping the law."
Topic: Brazil + civil law system (non-defining)
"Brazil, which follows a civil law system, relies on codified statutes rather than judicial precedent."
Chapter 6 — Your Turn
The Discussion
Guided → Semi-free → Free Production
Chapter 6 — Your Turn
Guided: Explain Brazilian Law to a Foreigner
Scenario: Conference Networking

An American attorney wants to understand how the Brazilian legal system works. Explain using at least 3 relative clauses and 5 vocabulary words.

judiciary codify statutory habeas corpus which
Chapter 6 — Your Turn
Semi-free: Panel Discussion
Scenario: Comparative Law Panel

You are on a panel comparing common law and civil law. The moderator asks you to discuss: tort law, arbitration, and the role of the judiciary. Use both defining and non-defining relative clauses.

plaintiff defendant liable arbitration common law
Chapter 6 — Your Turn
Free: Policy Debate
Scenario: Should Brazil Adopt Elements of Common Law?

Argue for or against: "Brazil should adopt elements of the common law system to strengthen its judiciary." Use at least 4 relative clauses and 6 vocabulary words. Consider: sovereignty, extradition, arbitration, and the role of precedent.

sovereignty amend extradite codify tort who/which
Chapter 7 — The Brief
Survival Card — Lesson 3
1 The judiciary, which interprets the law, plays a crucial role in both systems.
2 A tort is a wrongful act for which the responsible party may be held liable.
3 The government refused to extradite the suspect, citing national sovereignty.
4 Habeas corpus protects individuals who are unlawfully detained by the state.
5 Both countries have amended their constitutions to include statutory protections for civil rights.
Chapter 7 — The Brief
What I Learned Today
I can explain the difference between common law and civil law systems.
I can use defining relative clauses (who/which/that) without commas.
I can use non-defining relative clauses (who/which) with commas.
I know that "that" is NEVER used in non-defining clauses.
I can use 10+ new legal vocabulary words confidently.
Chapter 7 — The Brief
Badge Earned!

The System — Lesson 3 Complete

Before Next Class

2. Use at least 4 defining and 4 non-defining relative clauses

3. Include at least 8 vocabulary words from today

4. Complete the Pre-class exercises for Lesson 4

Coming Next

Lesson 3 — Complete
The System

General + Legal English — B2 Track

Chapter 1 — The Dilemma
The Dilemma Room

Lesson 4 — Hypothetical Legal Scenarios

Chapter 1 — The Dilemma
Warm-up: What If?

1. If you could change one law in Brazil, which one would you change?

2. If you were a judge, would you prefer to focus on punishment or rehabilitation?

3. If a friend asked you for legal advice, would you help or tell them to hire a lawyer?

Chapter 1 — The Dilemma
Quick Review
Verdict
Prosecution
Tort
Liability

From Lessons 1-3 — Do you remember?

Chapter 2 — Packing Words
New Vocabulary

13 words for criminal justice & ethics

Chapter 2 — Packing Words
Reveal the Words
Early release from prison under supervision
Parole
Early release from prison under supervision
"The prisoner was granted parole after serving ten years."
Supervised release instead of prison
Probation
Supervised release instead of prison
"The judge ordered two years of probation."
A serious crime — more than one year in prison
Felony
A serious crime — more than one year in prison
"Armed robbery is classified as a felony."
A minor crime with lighter punishment
Misdemeanor
A minor crime with lighter punishment
"Shoplifting under fifty dollars is a misdemeanor."
0 / 4 words revealed
Chapter 2 — Packing Words
More Words
A penalty for breaking a law or rule
Sanction
A penalty for breaking a law or rule
"The community imposed sanctions against the regime."
Following rules, laws, or regulations
Compliance
Following rules, laws, or regulations
"Institutions must ensure compliance with the law."
Involving deception for personal gain
Fraudulent
Involving deception for personal gain
"The executive was charged with fraudulent practices."
Failure to take reasonable care
Negligence
Failure to take reasonable care
"Medical negligence can result in compensation claims."
To pay someone for loss or damage
Compensate
To pay someone for loss or damage
"The court ordered the company to compensate the victims."
0 / 5 words revealed
Chapter 2 — Packing Words
Final Words
Something that discourages a particular action
Deterrent
Something that discourages a particular action
"Imprisonment serves as a deterrent against crime."
Restoring someone to normal life after prison
Rehabilitation
Restoring someone to normal life after prison
"The program helped inmates reintegrate into society."
Ensuring laws are obeyed
Enforcement
Ensuring laws are obeyed
"Strict enforcement of traffic laws reduced accidents."
Mercy or leniency shown to a convicted person
Clemency
Mercy or leniency shown to a convicted person
"The governor granted clemency after new evidence."
0 / 4 words revealed
Chapter 3 — The Code
The Code

Discover the grammar pattern

Chapter 3 — The Code
Look at the Pattern

If a prisoner showed genuine rehabilitation, the parole board would grant early release.

If we reduced sentences for misdemeanors, we would have more resources for enforcement.

If the governor granted clemency, the inmate would be released from death row.

If stricter sanctions were imposed, companies would improve their compliance programs.

What do the orange words have in common?

Chapter 3 — The Code
The Rule

Second Conditional

IF clauseIf + subject + past simple
Result clausesubject + would + infinitive
UseHypothetical, imaginary, or unlikely situations (present/future)

Note: "were" is used for all subjects in formal English ("If I were...", "If he were...")

Chapter 3 — The Code
Common Mistake
✗

"If I would have more evidence, I would win the case."

✓

"If I had more evidence, I would win the case."

NEVER use "would" in the IF clause!

Chapter 3 — The Code
Complete the Sentences

If the court ___ negligence, it would order compensation.

found

If rehabilitation ___ prioritized, society would benefit.

were

Companies would improve compliance if sanctions ___.

were stricter

If fraudulent behavior ___ unpunished, trust would erode.

went

Chapter 4 — The Workshop
The Workshop

Dialogue & Listening

Chapter 4 — The Workshop
Ethics Workshop Dialogue
KW

Dr. Karen Webb

Good afternoon, everyone. Welcome to the ethics workshop. I am Dr. Karen Webb from Columbia Law School.

LB

Luiz Bressane

Thank you, Dr. Webb. I am Luiz Bressane, a public defender from São Paulo.

KW

Dr. Karen Webb

Today we will explore hypothetical legal dilemmas. Let me start with a scenario. If a client confessed to you privately, but pleading guilty would result in a felony conviction, what would you do?

LB

Luiz Bressane

That is a classic ethical dilemma. If I were in that situation, I would advise my client on the consequences of both options, but I would never reveal a confidential confession.

KW

Dr. Karen Webb

Interesting. And if the prosecution offered probation instead of prison, would you recommend accepting the plea?

LB

Luiz Bressane

It would depend on the circumstances. If the evidence were weak, I would recommend going to trial. But if the case were strong, I would explain that probation is far better than a felony conviction.

KW

Dr. Karen Webb

Let us consider another scenario. If you discovered that a witness were providing fraudulent testimony, how would you handle it?

LB

Luiz Bressane

If I discovered fraudulent testimony, I would report it to the court immediately. Negligence in addressing fraud would undermine the entire justice system.

KW

Dr. Karen Webb

Now, here is the big question. If you could change one thing about the criminal justice system, what would it be?

LB

Luiz Bressane

If I could change one thing, I would invest more in rehabilitation programs. I believe that rehabilitation, not just punishment, is the most effective deterrent against crime.

Chapter 4 — The Workshop
Comprehension Check

1. What would Luiz do if a client confessed privately?

He would advise his client on the consequences but would never reveal a confidential confession.

2. What would Luiz do if he discovered fraudulent testimony?

He would report it to the court immediately.

3. If Luiz could change one thing about the justice system, what would it be?

He would invest more in rehabilitation programs, because rehabilitation is the most effective deterrent against crime.

Chapter 4 — The Workshop
Listening 1 — The Corporate Fraud Scenario
0:000:00

1. What would the judge likely do if the negligence were not intentional?

Reduce the charge to a misdemeanor.

2. What does the defense argue about parole and probation?

That they would be more appropriate than prison, citing the defendant's clean record.

Chapter 4 — The Workshop
Listening 2 — Three Perspectives on Reform
0:000:00

1. According to Rosa Mendes, what would happen if countries invested more in rehabilitation?

Recidivism rates would drop significantly.

2. What does Dr. Osei suggest about parole reform?

That if parole systems were reformed to include mandatory rehabilitation, fewer felons would reoffend.

Chapter 4 — The Workshop
Discussion

Which perspective do you agree with?

Rosa Mendes

"If countries invested more in rehabilitation, recidivism would drop."

James Whitfield

"If sanctions were more severe, compliance would improve naturally."

Amara Osei

"If parole systems were reformed to include rehabilitation, fewer felons would reoffend."

Chapter 4 — The Workshop
Pronunciation Drilling

If a prisoner showed genuine rehabilitation, the parole board would grant early release.

If we reduced sentences for misdemeanors, we would have more resources for enforcement.

If the governor granted clemency, the inmate would be released from death row.

If stricter sanctions were imposed, companies would improve their compliance programs.

Chapter 5 — Practice
Practice

Quick Fire · Spot the Error · Drilling

Chapter 5 — Practice
Quick Fire

Question 1 of 6

Dr. Webb asks: "If a company failed to report fraudulent activity, what would happen?" Answer using the second conditional.

"If a company failed to report fraudulent activity, regulators would impose heavy sanctions and the company would face criminal prosecution."

0 / 6

Chapter 5 — Practice
Spot the Error

0 / 5 errors found

"If I would have more evidence, I would win the case."
"If I had more evidence, I would win the case." (NEVER use "would" in the IF clause)
"If the defendant pleads guilty, he would receive probation."
"If the defendant pleaded guilty, he would receive probation." (Second conditional = past simple in IF clause)
"If enforcement agencies will have more resources, compliance would improve."
"If enforcement agencies had more resources, compliance would improve." (No "will" in IF clause of second conditional)
"If the governor grants clemency, the inmate would be released."
"If the governor granted clemency, the inmate would be released." (Hypothetical = past simple, not present)
"If negligence was proved, the company will compensate the victims."
"If negligence were proved, the company would compensate the victims." (Formal: "were" for all subjects; result = would, not will)
Chapter 5 — Practice
Transform: First to Second Conditional

First: "If we invest in rehabilitation, crime will decrease."

Second: "If we invested in rehabilitation, crime would decrease."

First: "If enforcement improves, compliance will follow."

Second: "If enforcement improved, compliance would follow."

First: "If the judge grants parole, the inmate will be free."

Second: "If the judge granted parole, the inmate would be free."

Chapter 5 — Practice
Build Your Sentences
CLEMENCY

Create a second conditional sentence about clemency.

FRAUDULENT

Create a second conditional sentence about fraud.

DETERRENT

Create a second conditional sentence about deterrents.

Chapter 6 — Your Turn
Your Turn

Role-play: Guided → Semi-free → Free

Chapter 6 — Your Turn
Role-play 1: Ethical Dilemma

Guided

You are at the ethics workshop. Dr. Webb presents this scenario: A corporation knowingly sold a fraudulent product that caused harm. The CEO claims negligence, not intent. What would you do as the defense attorney?

negligence compensate sanction compliance
Chapter 6 — Your Turn
Role-play 2: Policy Change

Semi-free

You are proposing a new policy at the workshop. Choose one: (A) Mandatory rehabilitation for all felony convictions, or (B) Replacing prison with probation for all misdemeanors. Present your argument using the second conditional.

rehabilitation enforcement deterrent parole
Chapter 6 — Your Turn
Role-play 3: Defense Strategy

Free

You are now the keynote speaker at the workshop. Present your vision for criminal justice reform in Brazil. Use at least 5 second conditional sentences and incorporate as many new vocabulary words as possible. Dr. Webb (teacher) will challenge you with questions.

clemency fraudulent sanction felony misdemeanor
Chapter 7 — The Resolution
The Resolution

Wrap-up & Reflection

Chapter 7 — The Resolution
Survival Card — Lesson 4
1

If fraudulent behavior went unpunished, it would undermine public trust in the justice system.

2

If probation programs were better funded, fewer offenders would return to crime.

3

If the court found negligence, the defendant would be ordered to compensate the victims.

4

If rehabilitation were prioritized over punishment, society would benefit in the long run.

5

If enforcement agencies had more resources, compliance rates would increase dramatically.

Chapter 7 — The Resolution
What I Learned
I can use the Second Conditional to discuss hypothetical scenarios.
I know 13 new words related to criminal justice and ethics.
I can distinguish between felony and misdemeanor.
I know NOT to use "would" in the IF clause.
I can present and defend hypothetical legal arguments.
Chapter 7 — The Resolution
Final Reflection

"If you could redesign the criminal justice system from scratch, what would be your three top priorities? Use the second conditional to explain."

Chapter 7 — The Resolution
Coming Next

Lesson 5 will explore how legal arguments are constructed and defended in formal debates.

The symposium continues...

Chapter 7 — The Resolution

General + Legal English — B2 Track

Chapter 1 — Roads Not Taken
The Turning Point
Career Decisions & Regrets
Third Conditional — If + past perfect, would have + past participle
Chapter 1 — Roads Not Taken
Warm-up Discussion
If you had not become a lawyer, what career would you have pursued?
Is there a case in your career that you wish you had handled differently?
Do you believe that one decision can change the entire course of a career?
Chapter 1 — Roads Not Taken
The Final Roundtable

It is the last day of the International Legal Symposium. You have been invited to a reflective roundtable led by retired Judge Margaret Torres.

Public defenders from around the world share career-defining moments and discuss what would have happened if they had made different choices.

Today, we look back at the roads not taken.

Chapter 2 — Packing Words
Legal Vocabulary
13 new words for reflecting on career-defining moments
Chapter 2 — Packing Words
Click to reveal each word
To formally declare someone not guilty
Acquit
To formally declare someone not guilty
"The jury voted to acquit the defendant."
To clear someone of blame after new evidence
Exonerate
To clear someone of blame after new evidence
"DNA evidence helped exonerate the wrongfully imprisoned man."
Unjust or not legally right
Wrongful
Unjust or not legally right
"The wrongful conviction was overturned on appeal."
To imprison or confine someone
Incarcerate
To imprison or confine someone
"Thousands of nonviolent offenders were incarcerated."
0 / 4 words revealed
Chapter 2 — Packing Words
More Words
To reduce a legal sentence to a less severe one
Commute
To reduce a legal sentence to a less severe one
"The governor commuted the life sentence to twenty years."
The tendency to reoffend after release
Recidivism
The tendency to reoffend after release
"Recidivism rates drop with education programs."
Compensation paid to a victim
Restitution
Compensation paid to a victim
"The court ordered restitution to the victims' families."
An official order to appear in court
Subpoena
An official order to appear in court
"The attorney issued a subpoena for documents."
A court hearing where the accused enters a plea
Arraignment
A court hearing where the accused enters a plea
"At the arraignment, he pleaded not guilty."
0 / 5 words revealed
Chapter 2 — Packing Words
Final Words
The crime of lying under oath in court
Perjury
The crime of lying under oath in court
"The witness was charged with perjury."
Disrespect for a court, punishable by fine
Contempt
Disrespect for a court, punishable by fine
"The judge held the attorney in contempt."
To make a formal judgment on a dispute
Adjudicate
To make a formal judgment on a dispute
"Three judges were appointed to adjudicate."
To officially erase a criminal record
Expunge
To officially erase a criminal record
"He applied to have his record expunged."
0 / 4 words revealed
Chapter 3 — The Code
Third Conditional
Imagining different outcomes for past events
Chapter 3 — The Code
Look at these sentences. What pattern do you see?

If the defense had presented stronger evidence, the jury would have acquitted the defendant.

If the witness had not committed perjury, the wrongful conviction would never have happened.

If the governor had commuted the sentence earlier, the inmate would have been exonerated sooner.

What do the highlighted words have in common?
Chapter 3 — The Code
The Rule
IF ClauseResult Clause
If + subject + had + past participlesubject + would have + past participle
If + subject + had not + past participlesubject + would not have + past participle
Question: Would + subject + have + past participle + if + subject + had + past participle?

We use the third conditional to talk about hypothetical situations in the PAST and their imagined results.

Chapter 3 — The Code
Common Mistake

If I had known, I would of helped.

"Would of" does NOT exist in English.

If I had known, I would have helped.

Contraction: I would've helped. (Sounds like "would of" but is NOT.)

If I would have known, I would have helped.

NEVER use "would have" in the IF clause. Use "had + past participle".

Chapter 3 — The Code
Complete the Third Conditional

If the record had been expunged___, the former inmate would have had a fresh start.

If the court had ordered restitution, the victims would have received___ compensation years ago.

If the arraignment had not been rushed___, the wrongful conviction could have been avoided.

Click each sentence to reveal the answer

Chapter 4 — The Roundtable
Roads Not Taken
A reflective dialogue between Luiz and Judge Margaret Torres
Chapter 4 — The Roundtable
Dialogue
Judge Torres Good afternoon, everyone. Welcome to our final roundtable. I am retired Judge Margaret Torres.
Luiz Thank you, Judge Torres. I am Luiz Bressane. It has been an extraordinary symposium.
Judge Torres Today I want us to reflect on the roads not taken. If you had made a different career choice, where would you be today?
Luiz That is a profound question. If I had not become a public defender, I would have pursued academia. But I would not have experienced the cases that shaped who I am.
Judge Torres I often think about a wrongful conviction case from my early career. If I had adjudicated that case differently, an innocent man would have been incarcerated for decades.
Luiz I had a similar experience. If the witness had not committed perjury, my client would never have been convicted. We fought for years to exonerate him.
Judge Torres If you had been the judge in that case, what would you have done differently?
Luiz If I had been the judge, I would have issued a subpoena for additional evidence before allowing the case to go to trial. The arraignment was rushed.
Judge Torres That is exactly why I advocate for having criminal records expunged after exoneration. If we had better systems, restitution would not take so long.
Luiz I completely agree. If the justice system had prioritized reducing recidivism through rehabilitation, we would have seen far fewer cases of contempt and repeat offenses.
Chapter 4 — The Roundtable
Dialogue Comprehension

1. What would Luiz have pursued if he had not become a public defender?

He would have pursued academia.

2. Why was Luiz's client wrongfully convicted?

Because the witness committed perjury (lied under oath).

3. What would Luiz have done differently if he had been the judge?

He would have issued a subpoena for additional evidence before allowing the case to go to trial.

Chapter 4 — The Roundtable
Listening 1 — Luiz's Career Reflection
0:00 / 0:00

1. How many years was Carlos incarcerated before being exonerated?

Six years.

2. What would have prevented the wrongful conviction?

If the witness had not committed perjury, and if the judge had issued a subpoena for additional surveillance footage.

Chapter 4 — The Roundtable
Listening 2 — Three Career Reflections
0:00 / 0:00

1. What would have happened if Judge Torres had not held the prosecution in contempt?

An innocent defendant would have been incarcerated for life.

2. According to Professor Ferreira, what would have reduced incarceration rates?

If countries had invested more in rehabilitation programs during the twentieth century.

Chapter 4 — The Roundtable
Listening Reflection

Using the third conditional, summarize what you heard about one of the three professionals:

Judge Torres

Wrongful conviction & contempt

David Park

Subpoena & restitution

Prof. Ferreira

Recidivism & rehabilitation

Example: "If Judge Torres had not held the prosecution in contempt, an innocent person would have been incarcerated for life."

Chapter 4 — The Roundtable
Key Vocabulary from the Dialogue
Perjury
lying under oath
Exonerate
clear of blame
Subpoena
official court order
Expunge
erase a record
Recidivism
reoffending
Contempt
disrespect for court
Chapter 4 — The Roundtable
Third Conditional in the Dialogue

1. If I had not become a public defender, I would have pursued academia.

2. If the witness had not committed perjury, my client would never have been convicted.

3. If I had been the judge, I would have issued a subpoena for additional evidence.

4. If the justice system had prioritized reducing recidivism, we would have seen fewer repeat offenses.

All describe imaginary PAST situations with hypothetical PAST results.

Chapter 5 — Practice
Quick Fire + Spot the Error
Testing your command of the third conditional
Chapter 5 — Practice
Quick Challenge
Question 1 of 6

Judge Torres asks: "If you had been assigned to a wrongful conviction case in your first year, how would you have handled it?" Answer using the third conditional.

"If I had been assigned to a wrongful conviction case in my first year, I would have fought to exonerate the defendant by issuing subpoenas for all available evidence."

0 / 6
Chapter 5 — Practice
Spot the Error
If the witness would have told the truth, the defendant would have been acquitted.
If the witness had told the truth, the defendant would have been acquitted.
If the governor had commuted the sentence, the inmate would of been released.
If the governor had commuted the sentence, the inmate would have been released.
If they had invested in rehabilitation, recidivism rates would decreased.
If they had invested in rehabilitation, recidivism rates would have decreased.
If the record had been expunged, he would has a fresh start.
If the record had been expunged, he would have had a fresh start.
If the judge had hold the attorney in contempt, the trial would have been fairer.
If the judge had held the attorney in contempt, the trial would have been fairer.

0 / 5 errors found

Chapter 5 — Practice
Listen and Repeat
If the defense had presented stronger evidence, the jury would have acquitted the defendant.
If the witness had not committed perjury, the wrongful conviction would never have happened.
If the governor had commuted the sentence, the inmate would have been exonerated sooner.
If the court had ordered restitution, the victims would have received compensation years ago.
Chapter 5 — Practice
Transform into Third Conditional

Fact: The arraignment was rushed. The conviction was wrongful.

If the arraignment had not been rushed, the wrongful conviction could have been avoided.

Fact: The attorney did not issue a subpoena. The evidence remained hidden.

If the attorney had issued a subpoena, the evidence would not have remained hidden.

Fact: The judge did not hold anyone in contempt. The trial was unfair.

If the judge had held the lawyer in contempt, the trial would have been fairer.

Chapter 6 — Your Turn
Role-Play Scenarios
Three levels: Guided → Semi-free → Free
Chapter 6 — Your Turn
Guided — Case Regret

Reflecting on a Past Case

Judge Torres asks you to share a case from your past where the outcome could have been different. Use the third conditional to explain what went wrong and what you would have done differently.

wrongful conviction subpoena perjury exonerate
Chapter 6 — Your Turn
Semi-free — Career Alternative

The Road Not Taken

Imagine you had chosen a completely different career path. Describe how your life would have been different. Where would you have lived? What would you have accomplished? What would you have missed?

incarcerate adjudicate commute restitution
Chapter 6 — Your Turn
Free — Policy Change Hypothetical

Rewriting History

If one major legal policy had been different in your country twenty years ago, how would the justice system look today? Discuss the impact on recidivism, wrongful convictions, incarceration rates, or any area of your choice. Speak freely for 2-3 minutes.

recidivism expunge arraignment contempt
Chapter 7 — The Legacy
Survival Card — Lesson 5
1 If the attorney had not issued the subpoena, the key evidence would have remained hidden.
2 If they had invested in rehabilitation, recidivism rates would have decreased significantly.
3 If the record had been expunged, the former inmate would have had a fresh start.
4 If the arraignment had not been rushed, the wrongful conviction could have been avoided.
5 If the judge had held the lawyer in contempt earlier, the trial would have proceeded more fairly.
Chapter 7 — The Legacy
What I Learned
I can use the third conditional to talk about hypothetical past situations.
I know the difference between "would have" and "would of".
I can use vocabulary like acquit, exonerate, wrongful, incarcerate, and commute.
I can use vocabulary like recidivism, restitution, subpoena, arraignment, perjury, contempt, adjudicate, and expunge.
I can reflect on past career decisions and express regret using the third conditional.

Badge Earned!

General + Legal English — B2 Track

Chapter 1 — The Question
The Scales
Defining Justice — Abstract Concepts
Mixed Conditionals
Chapter 1 — The Question
Warm-up Discussion
In one word: what is justice?
Do you believe the purpose of punishment is retribution, rehabilitation, or something else?
If the legal system had been designed differently from the start, how would society be different today?
Chapter 1 — The Question
A Philosophical Encounter

You have been invited to a virtual roundtable on the philosophy of justice with Professor Amara Osei, a renowned philosophy of law scholar from the University of Ghana.

Together, you will explore the tension between retribution and restoration, and whether justice can truly be impartial.

Today, we weigh the scales.

Chapter 2 — Words of Justice
Legal Vocabulary
13 new words for defining abstract concepts of justice
Chapter 2 — Words of Justice
Click to reveal each word
Fairness in treatment under the law
Equity
Fairness in treatment under the law
"True equity requires treating people according to their circumstances."
Not favoring one side; unbiased
Impartial
Not favoring one side; unbiased
"A judge must remain impartial throughout the trial."
The obligation to accept responsibility
Accountability
The obligation to accept responsibility
"Accountability is essential for public trust in the legal system."
Corresponding in size or degree
Proportional
Corresponding in size or degree
"The sentence should be proportional to the severity of the crime."
0 / 4 words revealed
Chapter 2 — Words of Justice
More Words
Punishment as payback for wrongdoing
Retribution
Punishment as payback for wrongdoing
"Some argue retribution is a necessary component of justice."
Focused on repairing harm, not punishing
Restorative
Focused on repairing harm, not punishing
"Restorative justice focuses on healing rather than punishment."
Too large or small in comparison
Disproportionate
Too large or small in comparison
"The punishment was disproportionate to the offense."
Existing as a natural or permanent quality
Inherent
Existing as a natural or permanent quality
"Human dignity is an inherent right."
Cannot be taken away or denied
Inalienable
Cannot be taken away or denied
"The constitution protects inalienable rights for all citizens."
0 / 5 words revealed
Chapter 2 — Words of Justice
Final Words
To formally end a system or practice
Abolish
To formally end a system or practice
"Many countries have voted to abolish the death penalty."
To maintain or support a law or principle
Uphold
To maintain or support a law or principle
"It is the duty of every court to uphold the rule of law."
Strong disagreement with an official decision
Dissent
Strong disagreement with an official decision
"Justice Oliveira filed a written dissent."
Of central importance; forming a foundation
Fundamental
Of central importance; forming a foundation
"Access to legal representation is a fundamental right."
0 / 4 words revealed
Chapter 3 — The Code
Mixed Conditionals
When past and present collide
Chapter 3 — The Code
Look at these examples. What pattern do you see?

If the court had abolished the death penalty, the system would be more humane today.

If the legislature had upheld the reform, citizens would have greater protections now.

If the punishment had been proportional, the public would trust the courts more today.

Notice: the IF clause is about the PAST, but the result is about NOW.

Chapter 3 — The Code
Mixed Conditionals

Type 1: Past → Present

If + had + past participle, would + infinitive

An unreal past action → a present result

"If the court had abolished the death penalty, the system would be more humane today."

Type 2: Present → Past

If + past simple, would have + past participle

An unreal present condition → a past result

"If justice were truly impartial, fewer wrongful convictions would have occurred."

Chapter 3 — The Code
Common Mistake

If the court had abolished the death penalty, the system would have been more humane today.

This is THIRD conditional (past → past). The word "today" tells us the result is NOW.

If the court had abolished the death penalty, the system would be more humane today.

MIXED conditional (past → present). Past action, present result.

If justice had been truly impartial, fewer wrongful convictions would have occurred.

This makes it third conditional. But the idea is that justice IS NOT impartial NOW.

If justice were truly impartial, fewer wrongful convictions would have occurred.

MIXED conditional (present → past). Present condition, past result.

Chapter 3 — The Code
Complete the Mixed Conditional

If the legislature had upheld___ the reform, citizens would have greater protections now.

If accountability were___ built into the system, fewer abuses would have gone unpunished.

If the dissenting opinion had prevailed, the law would protect___ more people today.

Click each sentence to reveal the answer

Chapter 3 — The Code
Error Correction

Teacher: type errors from the lesson below

Click here and type student errors...
Chapter 4 — Voices
The Debate
A philosophical dialogue between Luiz and Professor Amara Osei
Chapter 4 — Voices
Dialogue
Luiz Good afternoon, Professor Osei. I have been looking forward to this conversation about the philosophy of justice.
Prof. Osei Thank you, Luiz. The question of what justice truly means has occupied philosophers for millennia. Let me begin with a provocation.
Prof. Osei If retribution were the only purpose of punishment, would our legal systems be more just or less just today?
Luiz That is a fascinating question. If we had built our systems entirely around retribution, I believe we would have created more cycles of violence rather than justice.
Prof. Osei Exactly. And if restorative justice had been adopted as the fundamental approach decades ago, communities would have healed rather than fractured.
Luiz I see your point. But can we truly abolish punishment entirely? If there were no accountability, people would not feel safe.
Prof. Osei You raise an inherent tension in the philosophy of law. If equity and proportionality were always upheld, perhaps punishment would feel less like retribution and more like justice.
Luiz As a public defender, I have witnessed disproportionate sentences destroy lives. If the system had prioritized rehabilitation, many of my clients would be contributing members of society today.
Prof. Osei That brings us to inalienable rights. If we truly believed that dignity were inalienable, we would never have allowed such systemic inequities to persist.
Luiz I filed a dissent in a landmark case last year arguing exactly that. If the majority had agreed, it would have transformed how we define justice in Brazil.
Chapter 4 — Voices
Dialogue Comprehension

1. According to Professor Osei, what would have happened if restorative justice had been adopted decades ago?

Communities would have healed rather than fractured.

2. What inherent tension does Professor Osei identify in the philosophy of law?

The tension between equity/proportionality and retribution/punishment.

3. What did Luiz do in a landmark case last year?

He filed a dissent arguing that inalienable rights must include the right to proportional punishment.

Chapter 4 — Voices
Listening 1 — Luiz on Justice
0:00 / 0:00

1. What does Luiz believe would be different if equity had been the fundamental principle?

The outcomes for marginalized communities would be radically different today.

2. What did Luiz argue in his dissent last year?

That inalienable rights must include the right to proportional punishment.

Chapter 4 — Voices
Listening 2 — Professor Osei on Global Justice
0:00 / 0:00

1. What does Professor Osei say about indigenous African models of justice?

They prioritize community healing over individual punishment, and if Western systems had incorporated them, criminal justice would look fundamentally different.

2. What role does dissent play according to Professor Osei?

Dissent is not a threat to justice but a fundamental safeguard of it.

Chapter 4 — Voices
Listening Reflection

Using mixed conditionals, summarize what you heard from one of the speakers:

Luiz Bressane

Equity, disproportionate sentencing & dissent

Prof. Amara Osei

Restorative justice & inalienable rights

Example: "If equity had been the foundation of the system from the beginning, disproportionate sentencing would not exist today."

Chapter 4 — Voices
Key Vocabulary from the Dialogue
Retribution
punishment as payback
Restorative
focused on healing
Equity
fairness in treatment
Inalienable
cannot be taken away
Dissent
strong disagreement
Accountability
obligation to accept responsibility
Chapter 4 — Voices
Mixed Conditionals from the Dialogue

Type 1 If the system had prioritized rehabilitation, many clients would be contributing members of society today.

Type 2 If we truly believed that dignity were inalienable, we would never have allowed such inequities to persist.

Type 1 If the majority had agreed, it would have transformed how we define justice in Brazil.

Chapter 5 — Practice
Quick Fire
Mixed Conditionals in Action
Chapter 5 — Practice
Quick Fire Challenge

Question 1 of 6

A colleague asks: "Why is the justice system still unfair?" Use a mixed conditional (past action, present result) to answer.

"If the system had prioritized equity from the start, it would be much fairer today."

0 / 6

Chapter 5 — Practice
Spot the Error

0 / 5 errors found

"If the court had abolished the death penalty, the system would have been more humane today."

"...the system would be more humane today." (past → present, not past → past)

"If justice had been truly impartial, fewer wrongful convictions would have occurred."

"If justice were truly impartial..." (present condition, not past)

"If accountability would be built into the system, fewer abuses would have gone unpunished."

"If accountability were built into the system..." (never "would" in the IF clause)

"If the legislature had upheld the reform, citizens would have had greater protections now."

"...citizens would have greater protections now." (present result, not "would have had")

"If restorative justice were the norm, many victims would find closure years ago."

"...many victims would have found closure years ago." (past result needs "would have + pp")

Chapter 5 — Practice
Complete the Mixed Conditional

If we had recognized these rights as inalienable from the start, the legal framework would be___ stronger now.

If equity were___ the foundation of the system, disproportionate sentencing would never have become so widespread.

If the dissenting opinion had prevailed___, the law would protect more people today.

Click each sentence to reveal the answer

Chapter 5 — Practice
Complete These Sentences Orally

1. If the Brazilian constitution had included stronger provisions for equity, ___ (present result).

2. If all judges were truly impartial, ___ (past result).

3. If the government had abolished harsh sentencing for nonviolent offenses, ___ (present result).

Chapter 6 — The Arena
Your Turn
From guided to free: debate the meaning of justice
Chapter 6 — The Arena
Guided

You are presenting your position at a legal philosophy conference. Complete each statement:

1. "If the justice system had prioritized equity, the outcomes for ___ today."

2. "If accountability were the norm, fewer ___ would have ___."

3. "If we had adopted restorative approaches, communities would ___."

Chapter 6 — The Arena
Semi-free

You are debating a colleague who believes retribution is the only valid form of justice. Argue for a balanced approach using these concepts:

proportional disproportionate fundamental uphold inherent

Use at least 2 mixed conditionals in your argument.

Chapter 6 — The Arena
Free Debate

Choose your position and defend it:

Retribution

"Justice demands proportional punishment."

Restoration

"Justice demands healing, not punishment."

Your teacher will argue the opposite position. Use mixed conditionals to support your argument.

Chapter 7 — Reflection
What I Learned
  • I can use mixed conditionals (past → present) correctly.
  • I can use mixed conditionals (present → past) correctly.
  • I can discuss abstract concepts of justice fluently.
  • I understand the difference between retribution and restorative justice.
  • I can debate legal philosophy using advanced vocabulary.
Chapter 7 — Reflection
Badge Earned

Mixed Conditionals Mastered
