General + Legal English — B2 Track

Chapter 1 — The Witness Stand
The Expert Witness
Giving Detailed Accounts
Narrative Tenses Review
Chapter 1 — The Witness Stand
Warm-up Discussion
Have you ever had to take a witness's deposition? What was the experience like?
Why is it important that a witness recounts events in chronological order?
What makes a witness account reliable or unreliable in your experience?
Chapter 1 — The Witness Stand
Your Account

Describe what happened from the moment you left home today.

Try to include: what you were doing, what had happened before, and what happened next.

Chapter 2 — Key Terms
Vocabulary Reveal

Click each card to reveal the word. 0 / 4 words revealed

A person who sees an event and can describe it in court.

Witness

A person who sees an event and can describe it in court.

"The witness identified the suspect from a lineup."

To give evidence or a statement under oath.

Testify

To give evidence or a statement under oath.

"The forensic expert was called to testify."

A description or report of events.

Account

A description or report of events.

"The victim gave a detailed account of the robbery."

The order in which events occurred.

Sequence

The order in which events occurred.

"The detective reconstructed the sequence of events."

Chapter 2 — Key Terms
Vocabulary Reveal

Click each card to reveal the word. 0 / 4 words revealed

To confirm or support a statement with evidence.

Corroborate

To confirm or support a statement with evidence.

"Security footage corroborated the witness's account."

A difference between two things that should be the same.

Discrepancy

A difference between two things that should be the same.

"The defense found a discrepancy between the statements."

Made under a formal oath; legally binding.

Sworn

Made under a formal oath; legally binding.

"The officer submitted a sworn statement."

Formal sworn testimony given outside of court.

Deposition

Formal sworn testimony given outside of court.

"The attorney took the witness's deposition."

Chapter 2 — Key Terms
Quick Match

"The defense found a __________ between the two witness statements."

discrepancy

"Security footage __________ the witness's version of events."

corroborated

"The attorney took the witness's __________ three weeks before the trial."

deposition

"The officer submitted a __________ statement describing the arrest."

sworn

Chapter 2 — Key Terms
Your Words

Use these words to describe a real case from your experience:

corroborate discrepancy sworn deposition
Chapter 3 — The Timeline
Building the Narrative
Past Simple · Past Continuous · Past Perfect
Chapter 3 — The Timeline
Grammar Discovery

Past Simple

The witness identified the suspect from the lineup.

Past Continuous

The witness was walking home when he heard a loud noise.

Past Perfect

She had already left the office when the police arrived.

Chapter 3 — The Timeline
The Rule
TenseUseSignal Words
Past SimpleMain events in sequencethen, next, after that
Past ContinuousBackground / interrupted actionwhile, when, as
Past PerfectEarlier past (before the main event)already, by the time, before, after
Chapter 3 — The Timeline
Common Mistake
INCORRECT

"The witness called the police before the suspect fled."

Both in Past Simple = unclear which happened first

CORRECT

"The witness had already called the police before the suspect fled."

Past Perfect + Past Simple = clear time sequence

Chapter 3 — The Timeline
Grammar Practice

"The defendant __________ (enter) the building while the guard __________ (check) the cameras."

entered / was checking

"By the time the ambulance __________ (arrive), the victim __________ (lose) consciousness."

arrived / had lost

"The suspect __________ (run) down the alley when the officer __________ (spot) him."

was running / spotted

Chapter 3 — The Timeline
Transform the Narrative

Make these sentences more detailed for a court statement:

SIMPLE:

"The witness called the police. The suspect left."

DETAILED:

"The witness had already called the police before the suspect fled the scene."

NOW YOU TRY:

"The detective interviewed witnesses. The forensic team collected evidence."

"While the detective was interviewing witnesses, the forensic team was collecting evidence nearby."

Chapter 4 — The Hearing
Courtroom Exchange
Luiz Bressane & Judge Rebecca Morrison
Chapter 4 — The Hearing
Courtroom Dialogue
RM

Judge Morrison

"Good morning, Mr. Bressane. Before we begin, please state your name and role for the record."

LB

Luiz

"Good morning, Your Honor. I am Luiz Bressane, public defender assigned to the case of State versus Andrade."

Chapter 4 — The Hearing
Courtroom Dialogue
RM

Judge Morrison

"Can you recount the sequence of events on the night in question, based on your client's testimony?"

LB

Luiz

"According to my client, he was walking home from work at approximately nine pm. While he was crossing Avenida Paulista, he noticed a commotion near the bank."

Chapter 4 — The Hearing
Courtroom Dialogue
RM

Judge Morrison

"And what happened next? Did your client approach the scene?"

LB

Luiz

"No, Your Honor. My client stated that he had already called the police before the suspect fled the scene. He was standing across the street when the officers arrived."

Chapter 4 — The Hearing
Courtroom Dialogue
RM

Judge Morrison

"The prosecution claims there is a discrepancy between your client's account and the security footage. How do you respond?"

LB

Luiz

"The footage corroborates my client's account. He was standing on the opposite sidewalk the entire time. The discrepancy the prosecution refers to is a matter of camera angle, not of fact."

Chapter 4 — The Hearing
Dialogue Check

1. What was Luiz's client doing at nine pm?

He was walking home from work and crossing Avenida Paulista.

2. What had the client done before the suspect fled?

He had already called the police.

3. How does Luiz explain the discrepancy?

He says it is a matter of camera angle, not fact. The footage corroborates his client's account.

Chapter 4 — The Hearing
Listening: Witness Statement
0:00 / 0:00
Chapter 4 — The Hearing
Listening Questions

1. Where was the client walking when the incident occurred?

Along Rua Augusta toward the metro station / crossing Avenida Paulista.

2. What had the client already done before the patrol car arrived?

He had already called the emergency number.

3. How does the defense explain the timing discrepancy?

The bank's security system was running four minutes behind.

Chapter 4 — The Hearing
Listening 2: Case Summary
0:00 / 0:00
Chapter 5 — Building the Statement
Practice
From facts to narrative
Chapter 5 — Building the Statement
Spot the Error

"The witness was identifying the suspect from the lineup yesterday."

identified — completed action, not in progress

"By the time the police arrived, the suspect already escaped."

had already escaped — Past Perfect needed for earlier action

"While the detective interviewed the witness, the phone rang."

was interviewing — Past Continuous for background action interrupted

Chapter 5 — Building the Statement
Quick Fire

SITUATION 1

A client tells you: "I left work at 8. I walked down the street. I saw a man running." Recount this using proper narrative tenses.

"My client had finished work at eight pm. He was walking down the street when he saw a man running."

SITUATION 2

Describe to a judge: the forensic team arrived, they found that someone had already tampered with the evidence, and rain was falling.

"When the forensic team arrived at the scene, they discovered that someone had already tampered with the evidence. Rain was falling heavily, which complicated the collection process."

Chapter 5 — Building the Statement
Sworn Statement

Civil Police of the State of São Paulo

SWORN WITNESS STATEMENT

Case No. 2024-SP-04721

I, Carlos Andrade, declare under oath that on the night of March 15th, I had finished my shift at a logistics company in Pinheiros at 8:30 pm. I was walking along Rua Augusta toward the metro station when I heard shouting from a nearby bank. I stopped on the opposite sidewalk. While I was watching, I noticed a man carrying a dark bag. I had already called the emergency number before the first patrol car arrived.

Witness: Carlos Andrade

Date: March 16, 2024

Officer: Inspector M. Oliveira

Chapter 5 — Building the Statement
Complete the Statement

"The witness testified that the defendant __________ (threaten) the victim before the incident."

had threatened

"While the jury __________ (deliberate), the judge __________ (review) the sworn statements."

was deliberating / reviewed

"By the time the forensic team __________ (finish), the rain __________ (already / destroy) some of the evidence."

finished / had already destroyed

Chapter 6 — Your Testimony
Role-play
Give your witness statement
Chapter 6 — Your Testimony
Guided Testimony

Scenario

You are presenting your client's account of events to Judge Morrison. Your client was at a restaurant when a fight broke out. He had finished dinner and was waiting for the bill.

witness account sequence was waiting had finished
Chapter 6 — Your Testimony
Semi-free Testimony

Scenario

A colleague witnessed a traffic accident outside the courthouse. The judge asks you to recount what your colleague told you, using narrative tenses to maintain chronological order.

recount corroborate reliable
Chapter 6 — Your Testimony
Free Testimony

Your Case

Choose a real or imagined case. Present a full witness statement to the court. Use all three narrative tenses and at least four vocabulary words from today.

The judge (your teacher) will ask follow-up questions.

Chapter 6 — Your Testimony
Error Correction

Teacher: type errors from the role-play here...

What I Learned

Lesson 7 Checklist

I can use narrative tenses to give a detailed account of events.
I can distinguish when to use Past Simple, Past Continuous, and Past Perfect.
I can use courtroom vocabulary (witness, testify, deposition, corroborate).
I can present a witness statement in chronological order.
I can identify discrepancies and corroborate accounts with evidence.
Chapter 7 — Reflection
Badge Earned
The Expert Witness — Giving Detailed Accounts

Narrative Tenses Mastered

Chapter 7 — Reflection
Before Next Class

Complete the Pre-class exercises for Lesson 7.

Explore the Complementary Resources.

Your teacher will share additional homework details.

Lesson 8 — Block 1 Review

The Final Panel

Block 1 Review & Assessment

The São Paulo International Legal Summit

Chapter 1 — The Dream
Block 1 Reflection

What is the most important thing you have learned about law in English over the past 7 lessons?

Use Present Perfect to describe your journey so far.

Chapter 1 — The Dream
Grammar Confidence Check

Rate your confidence: 1 (need review) to 5 (confident)

Present Perfect Simple1 2 3 4 5
Present Perfect Continuous1 2 3 4 5
Mixed Tenses1 2 3 4 5
Defining & Non-defining Relative Clauses1 2 3 4 5
Second Conditional1 2 3 4 5
Third & Mixed Conditionals1 2 3 4 5
Narrative Tenses1 2 3 4 5
Chapter 2 — Packing Words
Block 1 Vocabulary Review

A formal declaration of innocence

Acquittal

A formal declaration that someone is not guilty of a charge.

"The jury returned a verdict of acquittal."

The duty to prove allegations in court

Burden of proof

The obligation to prove what is alleged in a legal proceeding.

"The burden of proof lies with the prosecution."

A previous case used as a standard

Precedent

A previous case used as a standard for future decisions.

"This ruling will set an important precedent."

The power of a court over a geographic area

Jurisdiction

The authority of a court to hear and decide cases in a specific area.

"This court does not have jurisdiction over federal matters."

0 / 4 words revealed
Chapter 2 — Packing Words
Block 1 Vocabulary Review

A formal written statement used as evidence

Deposition

Sworn testimony taken outside of court, used as evidence.

"The witness gave a deposition before the trial."

To confirm or support with evidence

Corroborate

To confirm or give support to a statement with additional evidence.

"The security footage corroborated the witness account."

Fair treatment through the legal system

Equity

Fairness and justice in the way people are treated by the law.

"The reform aims to promote equity in sentencing."

Cannot be taken away or denied

Inalienable

Rights that cannot be taken away, denied, or transferred.

"Access to legal representation is an inalienable right."

0 / 4 words revealed
Chapter 2 — Packing Words
Which lesson did we learn this?
Acquittal
Lesson 1
Precedent
Lesson 2
Deposition
Lesson 7
Equity
Lesson 6
Jurisdiction
Lesson 1
Corroborate
Lesson 7
Burden of proof
Lesson 1
Inalienable
Lesson 6

Click each word to reveal its lesson of origin

Chapter 2 — Packing Words
Words in Context

The defense argued that the evidence was insufficient, and the jury returned a verdict of acquittal___.

If the court had established___ a stronger precedent, similar cases would be handled differently today.

The new evidence helped to corroborate___ the witness testimony.

Access to legal representation is an inalienable___ right that cannot be denied.

Click each sentence to reveal the word

Chapter 3 — The Code
Name the Grammar Structure

"I have worked as a public defender for over fifteen years."

Present Perfect Simple

"I have been preparing for this conference since January."

Present Perfect Continuous

"While we were discussing the case, the judge announced a recess."

Mixed Tenses (Past Continuous + Past Simple)

"The lawyer, who specializes in human rights, presented a compelling argument."

Non-defining Relative Clause

"If I were the judge, I would rule in favor of the defendant."

Second Conditional

"If the reform had passed, thousands of inmates would have been released."

Third Conditional

"He was walking to court when he realized he had forgotten the file."

Narrative Tenses (Past Continuous + Past Simple + Past Perfect)

Click each sentence to reveal the structure

Chapter 3 — The Code
Present Perfect or Past Simple?

I have defended___ over 300 cases in my career. (defend)

Last year, the Supreme Court overturned___ the decision. (overturn)

The public defender's office has been struggling___ with funding for years. (struggle)

We filed___ the appeal on March 15th. (file)

Click each sentence to reveal the answer

Chapter 3 — The Code
Conditionals Review

If I were___ the prosecutor, I would approach this case differently. [2nd]

If the witness had testified___ earlier, the verdict would have been different. [3rd]

If the legislature had passed the reform, citizens would have___ greater protections now. [Mixed]

If the defendant had not confessed___, the trial would still be ongoing. [3rd]

Click each sentence to reveal the answer

Chapter 3 — The Code
Defining or Non-defining?

Does the relative clause need commas?

"The lawyer who represented the defendant won the case."

Defining (no commas) — identifies WHICH lawyer

"Dr. Silva, who has been a judge for 20 years, announced her retirement."

Non-defining (with commas) — extra information about Dr. Silva

"The case that changed constitutional law was decided in 1954."

Defining (no commas) — identifies WHICH case

"The Supreme Court, which is the highest court in the country, will hear the appeal."

Non-defining (with commas) — everyone knows which court

Click each sentence to reveal the answer

Chapter 3 — The Code
Spot the Error

I am working as a public defender since 2010.

I have been working as a public defender since 2010. (Present Perfect Continuous for duration)

If I would be the judge, I would dismiss the case.

If I were the judge, I would dismiss the case. (Second Conditional: were, not would be)

The defendant which was arrested last night has been released.

The defendant who was arrested last night has been released. (who for people, which for things)

If the evidence was presented earlier, the outcome would have changed.

If the evidence had been presented earlier, the outcome would have changed. (Third Conditional: had + past participle)

Click each sentence to reveal the correction

Chapter 4 — Getting There

The Conference Hall

Listening & Dialogue

Chapter 4 — Getting There
Listening 1 — Keynote Speech
0:00 / 0:00

Listen carefully. Comprehension questions follow.

Chapter 4 — Getting There
Listening 1 — Comprehension

1. What grammar structures did the speaker use to describe his career trajectory?

Present Perfect Simple and Continuous to describe ongoing experience and achievements.

2. What hypothetical reform did the speaker discuss, and which conditional type did he use?

He discussed sentencing reform using Third and Mixed Conditionals to explore what could have been different.

3. How did the speaker use narrative tenses when recounting a specific case?

He used Past Simple for key events, Past Continuous for background context, and Past Perfect for earlier actions.

Chapter 4 — Getting There
Listening 2 — Panel Discussion
0:00 / 0:00

Listen for relative clauses, conditionals, and narrative tenses.

Chapter 4 — Getting There
Listening 2 — Comprehension

1. What did the first panelist argue about restorative justice using conditionals?

If restorative justice had been adopted earlier, recidivism rates would be significantly lower today (Mixed Conditional).

2. How did the second panelist use relative clauses to describe legal systems?

She used non-defining relative clauses to add context: "The Nordic model, which prioritizes rehabilitation, has shown remarkable results."

3. What narrative tenses did the third panelist use to recount a landmark case?

Past Simple for key events, Past Perfect for earlier context, and Past Continuous for background actions during the trial.

Chapter 4 — Getting There
Conference Dialogue
LB

Luiz

"Dr. Obi, I have admired your work on international human rights law for years. It is a privilege to share this panel with you."

AO

Dr. Obi

"Thank you, Luiz. I have been researching comparative legal systems, and the work that public defenders do in Brazil is truly remarkable."

LB

Luiz

"If our legal system had adopted restorative practices earlier, many communities would be in a much better position today."

AO

Dr. Obi

"Absolutely. In Nigeria, which has a dual legal system, we have been experimenting with community courts that prioritize reconciliation over punishment."

Chapter 4 — Getting There
Conference Dialogue
LB

Luiz

"I remember a case last year. I was preparing the defense when new evidence emerged. The witness had given a contradictory deposition, which completely changed the direction of the trial."

AO

Dr. Obi

"That is fascinating. If every jurisdiction were to adopt mandatory deposition review protocols, cases like that would be resolved much more efficiently."

LB

Luiz

"I agree. The precedent that case set has influenced how our office handles similar situations. We have been training younger defenders to identify inconsistencies early."

AO

Dr. Obi

"That is exactly the kind of systemic change we need. The right to equity, which is inalienable, must be at the center of every legal reform."

Chapter 5 — Practice
Quick Fire
1 / 6

"She has been practicing law since she graduated."

What grammar structure is this?

Present Perfect Continuous

"The evidence that was presented yesterday contradicted the alibi."

What grammar structure is this?

Defining Relative Clause

"If the defendant had cooperated, the sentence would have been lighter."

What grammar structure is this?

Third Conditional

"While the jury was deliberating, the media reported on the case."

What grammar structure is this?

Mixed Tenses (Past Continuous + Past Simple)

"If I had more resources, I would take on more pro bono cases."

What grammar structure is this?

Second Conditional

"I have handled cases that involve constitutional rights."

What grammar structure is this?

Present Perfect Simple + Defining Relative Clause
Chapter 5 — Practice
Complete the Sentences

The defense team has been working___ on this appeal for over six months. (work — duration)

While the judge was reading___ the verdict, the defendant collapsed___. (read / collapse)

By the time the police arrived, the suspect had already fled___ the scene. (flee — earlier action)

Click to reveal

Chapter 5 — Practice
Complete the Sentences

If the constitution were___ amended, access to justice would improve___ dramatically. (2nd Conditional)

The Supreme Court, which has the final authority___, overturned the lower court ruling. (Non-defining)

If the key witness had not recanted___ her testimony, the defendant would have been convicted___. (3rd Conditional)

Click to reveal

Chapter 5 — Practice
Spot the Error

I have been a public defender for 2010.

I have been a public defender since 2010. (since + point in time, for + duration)

The lawyer which presented the case was very experienced.

The lawyer who presented the case was very experienced. (who for people)

If I would have known about the new evidence, I would have changed my strategy.

If I had known about the new evidence, I would have changed my strategy. (3rd Conditional: had + past participle in the if-clause)

He was already leaving when the police arrived. The crime happened 30 minutes before.

He had already left when the police arrived. The crime happened 30 minutes before. (Past Perfect for completed action before another past event)

I work on this case for three weeks now.

I have been working on this case for three weeks now. (Present Perfect Continuous for ongoing action)

Click each sentence to reveal the correction

Chapter 5 — Practice
Sentence Transformation

Past Simple → Present Perfect

"I started my career in 2010."

→ "I have worked in this field since 2010."

Second Conditional → Third Conditional

"If I were the judge, I would grant bail."

→ "If I had been the judge, I would have granted bail."

Defining → Non-defining Relative Clause

"The lawyer who won the case studied at Harvard."

→ "Ms. Torres, who studied at Harvard, won the landmark case."

Past Simple → Narrative Tenses

"The witness arrived and testified."

→ "The witness, who had been waiting outside, arrived just as the judge was calling a recess."

Click each to reveal the transformation

Chapter 6 — Your Turn

Your Panel

From guided to free production

Chapter 6 — Your Turn
Guided Panel

Conference Panel

A moderator (your teacher) asks you about your career and experience as a public defender. Use Present Perfect for your experience and Narrative Tenses to recount a specific case.

Required structures: Present Perfect + Narrative Tenses

15 years 300+ cases acquittal precedent
Chapter 6 — Your Turn
Panel Q&A

Audience Q&A

Audience members (your teacher) ask challenging questions about justice reform. Use Conditionals for hypothetical scenarios and Relative Clauses to add detail to your arguments.

Required structures: Conditionals + Relative Clauses

reform equity
Chapter 6 — Your Turn
Conference Speech

Final Assessment

Deliver a 3-minute speech at the summit. Cover your career, a key case, and your vision for justice reform. Use all 7 grammar structures from Block 1.

Present Perfect (Simple + Continuous) | Mixed Tenses | Relative Clauses | 2nd / 3rd / Mixed Conditionals | Narrative Tenses

Teacher: type errors from the speech here...

Chapter 7 — The Briefcase

The Briefcase

Wrap-up & Reflection

Chapter 7 — The Briefcase
Survival Card
1 "I have worked as a public defender since 2010."
2 "If the reform had passed, the system would be more equitable today."
3 "The judge, who has served for over 30 years, announced her retirement."
4 "While I was reviewing the case, new evidence emerged."
5 "If I were to redesign the legal system, I would prioritize equity above all else."
What I Learned

Lesson 8 Checklist

I can use all 7 grammar structures from Block 1 with confidence.
I can distinguish between Present Perfect Simple and Continuous.
I can use Second, Third, and Mixed Conditionals in legal contexts.
I can use defining and non-defining relative clauses correctly.
I can deliver a conference speech using varied grammar structures fluently.
Chapter 7 — The Briefcase
Badge Earned
The Final Panel — Block 1 Complete

All 7 Grammar Structures Mastered

Chapter 7 — The Briefcase
Block 1 Achievement
8
Lessons Completed
7
Grammar Structures
50+
Legal Terms Learned
8
Badges Earned

From courtroom basics to conference panels, you have built a strong foundation in Legal English. You can now discuss cases, argue hypotheticals, and present at international forums.

Chapter 7 — The Briefcase
Coming Up: Block 2

Legal English Deep Dive

Lessons 9-16 will cover:

Advanced argumentation and persuasion

Legal document drafting and analysis

Negotiation and mediation language

Complex case presentations

Chapter 7 — The Briefcase
Before Next Class

Complete the Pre-class exercises for Lesson 8.

Explore the Complementary Resources.

Your teacher will share additional homework details.

Complementary Resources — Aula 01

Série
Suits — Season 1, Episode 1: "Pilot"

Legal drama set in a top New York law firm. Observe how attorneys use formal and informal register, negotiate with clients, and argue in court. Excellent exposure to legal vocabulary in context.

Dica: Assista com legenda em inglês. Anote 5 expressões jurídicas que você reconheça da aula.

Podcast
Lawfare Podcast — "The Role of the Public Defender"

This podcast features discussions on criminal justice policy in the United States. Listen to how legal professionals describe their experience and argue their positions — great for B2 listening comprehension.

Dica: Ouça a 0.75x na primeira vez. Depois ouça a 1x e tente acompanhar sem pausar.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
TED Talk — Bryan Stevenson: "We need to talk about an injustice"

A powerful talk by a renowned criminal defense attorney about justice, mercy, and the failures of the legal system. Notice how he uses present perfect to describe his experiences and the impact of his work.

Dica: Ative as legendas em inglês. Anote como ele usa "I have seen..." e "We have been..." para descrever experiências.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Complementary Resources — Aula 02

Série
How to Get Away with Murder — Season 1, Episode 1

A legal drama centered on a brilliant criminal defense professor and her students. Excellent exposure to court vocabulary: prosecution, witness testimony, circumstantial evidence, sentencing, and appeals. Observe how characters narrate past events and describe ongoing cases.

Dica: Assista com legenda em inglês. Anote como os personagens usam past simple para descrever eventos específicos e present perfect para conquistas.

Podcast
Serial — Season 1 (Episodes 1-3)

A groundbreaking true-crime podcast investigating the 1999 murder of Hae Min Lee. Rich in legal vocabulary: witness testimony, circumstantial evidence, custody, bail, allegations, and appeals. The narrator uses mixed tenses extensively to describe past events and their present-day implications.

Dica: Ouça a 0.75x na primeira vez. Preste atenção em como a narradora alterna entre past simple e present perfect ao contar a história.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
Making a Murderer — Key Legal Moments (compilation)

A documentary series about Steven Avery's case, featuring real courtroom footage with appeals, sentencing hearings, and circumstantial evidence debates. Watch how attorneys, witnesses, and judges use formal legal language in real proceedings.

Dica: Ative as legendas em inglês. Anote exemplos de: "The court dismissed...", "He was convicted...", "The defense has filed an appeal..."

Watch on YouTube ↗

Complementary Resources — Aula 03

Série
Suits — Season 1, Episode 1: "Pilot"

A legal drama set in a top New York law firm operating under the common law system. Observe how attorneys argue cases using precedent, how the judiciary operates, and the dynamics between plaintiff and defendant in courtroom scenes. Excellent for understanding common law culture.

Dica: Assista com legenda em inglês. Anote como os advogados usam relative clauses para adicionar detalhes: "The client, who was accused of fraud, denied all charges."

Podcast
Radiolab — More Perfect

A podcast exploring landmark US Supreme Court decisions and constitutional law. Episodes cover topics like sovereignty, habeas corpus, and how the judiciary shapes American life. Rich in complex legal vocabulary and relative clauses used naturally by legal scholars.

Dica: Ouça a 0.75x na primeira vez. Preste atenção em como os especialistas usam "which" para adicionar informações extras sobre casos e leis.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
Common Law vs Civil Law — Explained

An educational video comparing the two major legal traditions in the world. Covers how common law relies on judicial precedent, how civil law codifies statutes, and where both systems converge (arbitration, habeas corpus). Perfect complement to the panel discussion in class.

Dica: Ative as legendas em inglês. Anote exemplos de: "Countries that follow common law..." vs "Civil law, which originated in Rome..."

Watch on YouTube ↗

Complementary Resources — Aula 04

Série
The Night Of — Season 1 (HBO)

A gripping miniseries about a young man accused of murder in New York. Rich in criminal justice vocabulary: felony charges, plea bargains, parole hearings, and ethical dilemmas. Observe how defense attorneys use hypothetical language to build their arguments and how the justice system handles compliance and enforcement.

Dica: Assista com legenda em inglês. Anote exemplos de linguagem hipotética: "If the evidence were stronger..." "Would the jury convict if...?"

Podcast
Criminal — Selected Episodes

A podcast telling real stories of crime, justice, and the people caught up in the system. Episodes explore themes of rehabilitation, clemency, enforcement failures, and ethical dilemmas. Excellent for hearing natural American English discussing criminal justice concepts in context.

Dica: Ouça a 0.75x na primeira vez. Preste atenção em como os entrevistados usam condicionais para especular: "If things had been different..." "What would have happened if...?"

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
Michael Sandel — Justice: What’s the Right Thing to Do?

A legendary Harvard lecture series where Professor Sandel presents hypothetical ethical scenarios and challenges students to reason through them. Perfect for understanding how hypothetical language is used in academic and legal contexts. Watch how students use "if... would..." to argue their positions.

Dica: Ative as legendas em inglês. Anote como Sandel formula perguntas hipotéticas: "If you were the judge, would you...?" "What if the law required...?"

Watch on YouTube ↗

Complementary Resources — Aula 05

Série
When They See Us (Ava DuVernay, 2019)

A powerful limited series about the wrongful conviction of five teenagers in New York. Explores themes of incarceration, perjury, exoneration, and the devastating consequences of a flawed justice system. Essential viewing for understanding the human impact of wrongful convictions and the fight for restitution.

Dica: Assista com legenda em inglês. Observe como os advogados usam linguagem hipotética: "If the police had followed proper procedure..." "If the evidence had been examined..."

Podcast
Wrongful Conviction — Selected Episodes

A podcast featuring real exoneration stories told by the people who lived them. Each episode explores how wrongful convictions occur through perjury, flawed arraignment procedures, and inadequate legal representation. Hear how individuals fought to have their records expunged and sought restitution after years of incarceration.

Dica: Ouça a 0.75x na primeira vez. Preste atenção no third conditional: "If the DNA evidence had been tested earlier..." "If the judge had issued the subpoena..."

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
Bryan Stevenson — We Need to Talk About Injustice (TED Talk)

Bryan Stevenson, founder of the Equal Justice Initiative, shares career-defining moments in his fight against mass incarceration and wrongful convictions. His powerful storytelling explores how the justice system could have been different and what it means to adjudicate with compassion. One of the most-watched TED Talks of all time.

Dica: Ative as legendas em inglês. Observe como Stevenson reflete sobre o passado: "If we had addressed these issues decades ago..." Anote 3 frases com third conditional ou linguagem reflexiva.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Complementary Resources — Aula 06

Série
The Good Fight — Season 3, Episode 1

A thought-provoking legal drama that explores the moral complexities of the American justice system. This episode examines questions of equity, accountability, and whether the system can truly be impartial. Characters frequently debate the tension between retribution and restorative approaches to justice.

Dica: Assista com legenda em inglês. Observe como os advogados usam condicionais mistas para debater hipóteses sobre o sistema judicial: "If the court had ruled differently..." "If the law were applied equally..."

Podcast
More Perfect (from Radiolab) — “The Imperfect Plaintiffs”

This episode explores how the Supreme Court selects cases and plaintiffs, raising fundamental questions about equity and inalienable rights. It examines cases where dissenting opinions later became the law of the land, showing how accountability and proportionality evolve over time.

Dica: Ouça a 0.75x na primeira vez. Preste atenção em como os apresentadores usam condicionais para especular: "If the court had taken this case instead..." "If these rights were truly inalienable..."

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
Michael Sandel — Justice: What’s the Right Thing to Do? (Harvard Lecture)

Professor Sandel's legendary lecture series where he presents ethical dilemmas and challenges students to reason through them. This lecture directly addresses the philosophical foundations of justice, including retribution vs restoration, proportional punishment, and inherent rights. Students use conditional language extensively to argue their positions.

Dica: Ative as legendas em inglês. Observe como Sandel formula perguntas usando condicionais: "If the punishment had been proportional..." "If we believed rights were truly inalienable..." Anote 3 mixed conditionals que você ouvir.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Complementary Resources — Aula 07

Série
The Staircase (Netflix) — Episode 1: “911”

A gripping true-crime docuseries that follows a murder trial from start to finish. This episode focuses on the initial witness statements and police accounts, showing how narrative tenses shape the retelling of events. Notice how witnesses recount what they saw, what was happening, and what had occurred before the incident.

Dica: Assista com legenda em inglês. Observe como as testemunhas alternam Past Simple, Past Continuous e Past Perfect ao descrever os eventos. Anote 3 frases que usem "was + -ing" e 3 que usem "had + past participle".

Podcast
Serial (Season 1) — Episode 2: “The Breakup”

Sarah Koenig investigates the case of Adnan Syed, examining witness testimony and timeline discrepancies. This episode dissects how different witnesses recount the same events in different sequences, and how reliability is questioned when accounts contain chronological gaps. The narrative is rich with past tenses.

Dica: Ouça a 0.75x. Preste atenção em como Koenig usa narrative tenses para reconstruir eventos: "He said he had been at the library when..." "She was driving when she noticed..." Anote 3 discrepancies mencionadas.

Listen on Serial ↗
YouTube
Elizabeth Loftus — How Reliable Is Your Memory? (TED Talk)

Cognitive psychologist Elizabeth Loftus presents decades of research on the unreliability of eyewitness testimony. She recounts famous cases where witnesses gave sworn accounts that were later proven false, and explains how memory reconstructs rather than replays events. The talk uses narrative tenses extensively to describe experiments and case studies.

Dica: Ative as legendas em inglês. Observe como Loftus usa narrative tenses para contar histórias de casos reais: "The witness had identified the wrong man..." "While the police were questioning him..." Anote 3 exemplos de Past Perfect.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Complementary Resources — Aula 08

Série
The Good Fight (Paramount+) — Season 1, Episode 1: “Inauguration”

A legal drama that covers virtually every structure from Block 1: courtroom cross-examinations, hypothetical legal arguments, formal presentations, and complex narrative accounts. The pilot episode introduces multiple legal proceedings simultaneously, making it ideal for reviewing how grammar shifts with context.

Dica: Assista com legenda em inglês. Identifique pelo menos: 1 Present Perfect, 1 Relative Clause, 1 Conditional, e 1 Narrative Tense sequence. Anote as frases.

Podcast
Radiolab — “More Perfect: The Imperfect Plaintiffs”

This episode from Radiolab's legal series examines how key court cases set precedents that changed American law. The narrative weaves between past events (narrative tenses), their ongoing impact (present perfect), and hypothetical alternatives (conditionals). A comprehensive listening exercise for Block 1 review.

Dica: Ouça a 0.85x. Classifique cada trecho que ouvir como: (a) narrative, (b) present perfect, (c) conditional, ou (d) relative clause. Quantos exemplos de cada você consegue identificar?

YouTube
Bryan Stevenson — We Need to Talk About an Injustice (TED Talk)

Civil rights lawyer Bryan Stevenson delivers a powerful talk about the American justice system. He uses Present Perfect to describe his career achievements, narrative tenses to recount specific cases, conditionals to discuss what could change, and relative clauses to define legal concepts. This single talk covers every grammar point from Block 1.

Dica: Ative legendas em inglês. Para cada estrutura gramatical do Block 1, encontre pelo menos 1 exemplo no discurso de Stevenson. Anote a frase e identifique a estrutura.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Série
The Night Of (HBO) — Season 1, Episode 2: “Subtle Beast”

A near-documentary look at criminal procedure: the arrest, the warrant, the bail hearing, the remand into custody. Narration and police reports are saturated with passive constructions (“the suspect was detained”, “bail was set”), exactly the structures from tonight’s lesson.

Dica: Assista com legenda em inglês e anote 8 frases na voz passiva. Para cada uma, escreva a versão ativa equivalente.

Podcast
More Perfect (WNYC Studios) — episode on a landmark criminal appeal

Long-form legal journalism that narrates how cases move through the system. Reporters lean heavily on the passive when the agent is unknown or unimportant (“the case was remanded”, “the ruling was overturned”), giving B2 listeners a natural model of register.

Dica: Ouça 15 minutos a 1x. Pause sempre que ouvir uma forma passiva e repita a frase em voz alta antes de continuar.

YouTube
BBC Learning English — “The Passive Voice” (English In A Minute / 6 Minute Grammar)

A concise, native-speaker review of be + past participle across tenses, with the difference between active and passive made explicit. A clean reinforcement of tonight’s grammar before the homework is recorded.

Dica: Assista, pause, e reescreva cinco frases do seu próprio trabalho na voz passiva. Grave o resumo de 2 minutos do dever de casa.

Série
The Staircase (Netflix) — Episode 3: “A Striking Coincidence”

A documentary trial where witnesses and experts contradict one another. Lawyers constantly relay testimony in reported speech (“the witness said that she had heard…”, “the expert claimed that the wound was…”), the exact structure from tonight’s lesson.

Dica: Assista com legenda em inglês e anote 8 frases em reported speech. Para cada uma, escreva a fala direta original que a pessoa provavelmente disse.

Podcast
Serial (This American Life) — Season 1, a witness-driven murder case

Long-form legal journalism built almost entirely on reporting what people said. The host constantly backshifts: “he told her that he had been at track practice”, “she said that she couldn’t remember”. A natural B2 model of say versus tell and tense shift.

Dica: Ouça 15 minutos a 1x. Pause sempre que ouvir um reporting verb (said, told, claimed, admitted) e repita a frase em voz alta antes de continuar.

YouTube
BBC Learning English — “Reported Speech” (English In A Minute / 6 Minute Grammar)

A concise, native-speaker review of backshift, say versus tell, and reporting questions. A clean reinforcement of tonight’s grammar before the homework is recorded.

Dica: Assista, pause, e reescreva cinco falas diretas de uma testemunha em reported speech. Grave o resumo de 2 minutos do dever de casa.
